You are a flag detection model for government RFP screening. Your job is to identify which flags, if any, are present in the provided RFP text chunk.

You will be given:
- A list of relevant flag definitions and examples (retrieved context)
- An RFP text chunk to analyze

Rules:
- Output one flag name per line, using only the exact flag names listed below
- If no flags are present, output: no_flag
- Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any other text

Valid flags:
waterfall_methodology, off_the_shelf_software, no_custom_development, lpta_source_selection,
small_business_set_aside, 8a_set_aside, wosb_set_aside, edwosb_set_aside, sdvosb_set_aside,
hubzone_set_aside, agile_methodology, oral_presentation, design_exercise, budget_too_low,
brownfield, onsite_required, onsite_madison, large_team, marginal_short_duration, no_flag

[Retrieved context]

Example 1:
A sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is justified under 10 USC 2304(c)(5), FAR 6.302-5, authorized or required by statute.

FAR Part 19.1306(a) specify when a sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is appropriate. This acquisition meets those circumstances, as follows:
a. The contracting officer does not have a reasonable expectation that offers would be received from two or more HUBZone small business concerns; Market research identified DH Technologies, LLC as the only HUBZone small business authorized distributor for these items;
b. The anticipated price of the contract, including options, will not exceed $6.5 million for a requirement within the NAICS codes for manufacturing or $4.5 million for a requirement, within any other NAICS code; the applicable NAICS code is 541519 so the $4.5 million limitation applies. As stated above, the estimated dollar value is $532,194 for the base year;
c. This is a new requirement that is not currently being performed by an 8(a) participant under the provisions of FAR subpart 19.8. No performance by an equal 8(a) participant was identified during market research;
d. The HUBZone small business concern has been determined to be a responsible contractor with respect to performance, DH Technologies, LLC has a history of successful performance on previous contracts for these items.

Example 2:
This is a COMBINED SYNOPSIS/SOLICITATION for commercial items prepared in accordance with the information in FAR Subpart 12.6, as supplemented with the additional information included in this notice. This solicitation is advertised as a 100% SDVOSB business set-aside. This announcement constitutes the only solicitation; a written solicitation will not be issued. PAPER COPIES OF THIS SOLICITATION WILL NOT BE AVAILABLE. This combined synopsis/solicitation SHALL be posted to SAM.gov https://sam.gov/content/home). The RFQ number is 93310026Q0004. This solicitation documents and incorporates provisions and clauses in effect through FAC 2023-06 11/06/2023. It is the responsibility of the contractor to be familiar with the applicable clauses and provisions. The FAR clauses may be accessed in full text at these addresses http://www.acquisition.gov/far/. The NAICS code is 541519 and the Small Business Standard is $34 Million. The Federal Mediation and Conciliation Service (FMCS) intends to award a firm fixed price purchase order to procure zoom for government (ZFG) Licenses. Please see Attachment 1 – Vendor Quote Sheet for the details.

Example 3:
This action will be a Firm Fixed-Price Contract to include software and associated success support with an estimated annual cost of $532,194 for a one (1) year base period with four (4) one-year option periods. The provider is a HUBZone small business set-aside directed to DH Technologies, LLC (CAGE: 6XKC9), 161 Fort Evans Road NE, Suite 255 Leesburg VA 20176.

The solicitation proposes to award an Indefinite Quantity Contract (IQC) for:
• Posit Package Manager Advanced
• Posit Connect Advanced - 200 Licenses Package
• Posit Workbench Advanced - 20 Licenses Package
• Posit Success Support

DH Technologies, LLC (CAGE: 6XKC9) is the only HUBZone small business source that is a Posit authorized distributor identified through market research that is capable of providing these part numbers. The contract will cover a Base Period of one year with four one year option periods for each item. The total estimated dollar value for the base year is $532,194.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
SECTION I – CONTRACT CLAUSES

The following Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) clauses are incorporated by reference and apply to this solicitation and any resulting contract:

52.219-3 Notice of HUBZone Set-Aside or Sole-Source Award (NOV 2020)
52.219-4 Notice of Price Evaluation Preference for HUBZone Small Business Concerns (OCT 2022)
52.219-8 Utilization of Small Business Concerns (OCT 2022)
52.219-14 Limitations on Subcontracting (OCT 2022)
52.219-28 Postaward Small Business Program Rerepresentation (MAR 2023)

This solicitation is issued as a total HUBZone small business set-aside pursuant to FAR 19.1305. Only offers from HUBZone small business concerns that are certified by the U.S. Small Business Administration (SBA) at the time of offer submission and that maintain their HUBZone certification through contract award will be considered for award. Offerors must be listed as a certified HUBZone small business concern in the SBA's Dynamic Small Business Search (DSBS) at the time of initial offer and at the time of award.

The applicable NAICS code for this requirement is 561210 – Facilities Support Services, with a size standard of $47.0 million. The HUBZone price evaluation preference factor of 10 percent shall apply in accordance with FAR 19.1307 when evaluating offers from HUBZone small business concerns against offers from other than HUBZone small business concerns, should the set-aside be withdrawn. Contractors must comply with FAR 52.219-14, Limitations on Subcontracting, ensuring that at least 50 percent of the cost of contract performance incurred for personnel shall be expended for employees of the HUBZone concern.

The U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service, Southeast Area, anticipates awarding a single Firm Fixed-Price contract for facilities maintenance and custodial support services at the Jamie Whitten Delta States Research Center in Stoneville, Mississippi. The period of performance includes a 12-month base period and four 12-month option periods.

RFP chunk to analyze:
JUSTIFICATION FOR OTHER THAN FULL AND OPEN COMPETITION
HUBZone Sole Source Award

1. Agency: Department of Veterans Affairs, Network Contracting Office 4, Pittsburgh, PA
2. Contracting Activity: Veterans Health Administration, VA Pittsburgh Healthcare System
3. Description of Action: Sole source award to a HUBZone small business concern for biomedical equipment preventive maintenance and repair services.
4. Authority: FAR 6.302-5(b)(5) – Set-asides for HUBZone small business concerns authorized under FAR 19.1306.

5. Basis for Sole Source Justification:

Pursuant to FAR 19.1306(a), a sole source HUBZone award may be made when the contracting officer does not have a reasonable expectation that offers would be received from two or more HUBZone small business concerns. Market research, including review of SAM.gov registrations, SBA's HUBZone certification database, and prior procurement history, identified MedServ Solutions, Inc. (DUNS: 078451239, CAGE: 8JKP2), located at 2490 Sidney Street, Suite 300, Pittsburgh, PA 15203, as the only certified HUBZone small business concern in the local area with the requisite OEM certifications and trained personnel to service the specific biomedical equipment models currently installed at VA Pittsburgh Healthcare System.

The anticipated total contract value, including options, is $3,850,000, which falls below the $4.5 million threshold for non-manufacturing NAICS codes as required by FAR 19.1306(a)(2). The applicable NAICS code is 811210 – Electronic and Precision Equipment Repair and Maintenance, with a size standard of $34.0 million.

MedServ Solutions, Inc. has previously performed similar work under Contract No. 36C24420C0087 and received satisfactory to exceptional performance ratings in the Contractor Performance Assessment Reporting System (CPARS). The firm has been determined to be a responsible contractor. The requirement is not currently being performed by an 8(a) participant under FAR Subpart 19.8.

100% SDVOSB SET ASIDE

be verified by the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) as SDVOSB (see https://www.veterans.certify.sba.gov/ for details)

and

RFP chunk to analyze:
C.4 VAAR 852.219-73 VA NOTICE OF TOTAL SET-ASIDE FOR CERTIFIED SERVICE-DISABLED VETERAN-OWNED SMALL BUSINESSES (JAN 2023) (DEVIATION)

(a) Definition. for the Department of Veterans Affairs, ''Service-disabled Veteran-owned small business concern or SDVOSB'':
(1) Means a small business concern—
(i) Not less than 51 percent of which is owned by one or more service-disabled Veterans or, in the case of any publicly owned business, not less than 51 percent of the stock of which is owned by one or more service-disabled Veterans or eligible surviving spouses (see VAAR 802.201, Surviving Spouse definition);
(ii) The management and daily business operations of which are controlled by one or more service-disabled Veterans (or eligible surviving spouses) or, in the case of a service-disabled Veteran with permanent and severe disability, the spouse or permanent caregiver of such Veteran;
(iii) The business meets Federal small business size standards for the applicable North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code identified in the solicitation document;
(iv) The business has been certified for ownership and control pursuant to 38 U.S.C. 8127, 13 CFR 128, and is listed as certified in the SBA certification database at https://veterans.certify.sba.gov/; and
(v) The business agrees to comply with VAAR subpart 819.70 and Small Business Administration (SBA) regulations regarding small business size, government contracting, and the Veteran Small Business Certification Program at 13 CFR parts 121, 125, and 128.

(b) General. In order for a concern to submit an offer and be eligible for the award of an SDVOSB set-aside or sole source contract, the concern must qualify as a small business concern under the size standard corresponding to the NAICS code assigned to the contract and be listed as an SDVOSB participant in the SBA certification database as set forth in 13 CFR 128.
(1) Offers received from entities that are not certified SDVOSBs and listed in the SBA certification database at the time of offer shall not be considered.
(2) Any award resulting from this solicitation shall be made to a certified SDVOSB listed in the SBA certification database who is eligible at the time of submission of offer(s) and at the time of award.

Example 1:
PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

Example 2:
4. EVALUATION FACTORS
As part of the Technical Evaluation, the NAFI may require an oral presentation or interview from the Contractor deemed LPTA. The outcome of the oral presentation or interview may lead to an adjustment in the Contractor's Technical Evaluation Rating. The Contractor will be provided at least three (3) days advance notice, including the presentation/interview requirements and questions.

The government will evaluate RFQ submission based on the following evaluation factors:
FACTOR 1: Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW)/Technical Solution
FACTOR 2: Past Performance
FACTOR 3: Cost/Price

4.1. FACTOR 1: Contractor's Statement of Work (CSOW) (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
A Contractor's quote is deemed technically acceptable when the contractor presents a viable CSOW that is clear and include sufficient detail for effective evaluation and for substantiating the validity of stated claims. The CSOW will include at a minimum a narrative addressing each paragraph in the Statement of Objectives (SOO) (specifically Paragraphs 3-6.12). The narrative will outline the Contractor's complete understanding of the requirement and how the contractor will fulfill all requirements.

The Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW) will be incorporated into the contract at the time of award. See the SOO, for the technical aspects.

Example 3:
9. FILE TWO – PRICING (Reference Supplies, Services and Prices & Evaluation Factors - LPTA for Award, Price Evaluation)
9.1. File Organization
9.1.1. Table of Contents
9.1.2. List of Tables and Figures
9.1.3. Incremental and Total Price Summary (ATCH 05)
9.1.3.1. Contractor will propose unit prices for each Contract Line Item (CLIN) – IAW Supplies, Services and Prices of this solicitation. Failure to provide prices as stated above might result in rejection of the quote. Total evaluated price will include the base and all option years.

10. FILE THREE – TECHNICAL SOLUTION (Statement of Objectives (SOO) & Evaluation Factors - LPTA for Award)
10.1. File Organization
10.1.1. Table of Contents File III
10.1.2. List of Tables & Figures for File III
10.1.3. Assumptions
10.1.4. Technical Solution/ Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW)
10.2. Technical Solution/CSOW
10.2.1. The Contractor will provide a CSOW. The CSOW defines/explains how the contractor will meet the tasks required to fulfill the requirements of the Statement of Objectives (SOO). The Contractor will provide a written response outlining its technical solution to execute the requirement outlined in each paragraph and subparagraph in the SOO. "Yes," "Met," and other ambiguous responses that do not provide an actual technical solution will be deemed unacceptable. The NAFI will evaluate the CSOW as part of Technical Evaluation (Evaluation Factors - LPTA). The CSOW will be incorporated into the contract award.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
E.9 52.212-2 EVALUATION—COMMERCIAL PRODUCTS AND COMMERCIAL SERVICES (NOV 2021)

(a) The Government will award a contract resulting from this solicitation to the responsible offeror whose offer conforming to the solicitation will be most advantageous to the Government, price and other factors considered. The following factors shall be used to evaluate offers:

Quotes will be evaluated on the following factors:
1. Technical Approach
2. Experience
3. Past Performance

Each factor will be evaluated and given a rating of Acceptable or Unacceptable. The Government will evaluate quotations using the comparative evaluation process where quotations will be compared to one another to determine which provides the best benefit to the Government. The Government reserves the right to consider a quotation other than the lowest price that provides additional benefit(s).

(b) Options. The Government will evaluate offers for award purposes by adding the total price for all options to the total price for the basic requirement. The Government may determine that an offer is unacceptable if the option prices are significantly unbalanced. Evaluation of options shall not obligate the Government to exercise the option(s).

(c) A written notice of award or acceptance of an offer, mailed or otherwise furnished to the successful offeror within the time for acceptance specified in the offer, shall result in a binding contract without further action by either party.

Example 1:
7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

Example 2:
6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

To be considered for this work, the contractor must be capable of providing an MSP&A integration / innovation lab, supporting approximately 60 personnel, in the Colorado Springs, CO area that is approved (or will be within the current government fiscal year) to operate up to TS/SCI and SAP levels. The capabilities at this facility should allow multiple vendors to collaborate on analytical objectives supporting the customer(s).

2.0 Background
The Space Warfighting Analysis Center (SWAC) and the Space Security and Defense Program (SSDP) provide force design recommendations to the USSF via analytical studies, leveraging multiple vendors, government civilians, and military personnel.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

Offerors shall provide a (1) proposed Deployment Approach (A003) (Volume 1: Factor 1 – Challenge Scenario 1a) as well as a (2) proposed Deployment Approach (A003) (Volume 1: Factor 1 – Challenge Scenario 1b) based on both of the following scenarios.

• One large accredited Outside Continental U.S. (OCONUS) medical center (250 beds), including two community hospitals (125 beds) and five associated out-patient clinics spread across multiple time zones with approximately 5,000 total users that includes foreign national employees and 50,000 beneficiaries.

Example 1:
7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

Example 2:
To be considered for this work, the contractor must be capable of providing an MSP&A integration / innovation lab, supporting approximately 60 personnel, in the Colorado Springs, CO area that is approved (or will be within the current government fiscal year) to operate up to TS/SCI and SAP levels. The capabilities at this facility should allow multiple vendors to collaborate on analytical objectives supporting the customer(s).

2.0 Background
The Space Warfighting Analysis Center (SWAC) and the Space Security and Defense Program (SSDP) provide force design recommendations to the USSF via analytical studies, leveraging multiple vendors, government civilians, and military personnel.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

Example 3:
The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

RFP chunk to analyze:
1b) OpMed products' modernization and deployment: This effort delivers mobile and interoperable OpMed solutions to medical forces in deployed and tactical environments in both the Continental U.S. (CONUS) and OCONUS. This includes theater hospitals, hospital ships, forward resuscitative sites, and submarines. These scalable deployments can occur concurrently across multiple sites, countries, or theaters, in remote or austere environments with each location receiving one or more products tailored to mission requirements. The deployment includes the OpMed product suite: Battlefield Assisted Trauma Distributed Observation Kit-Joint (BATDOK-J), Operational Medicine Care Delivery Platform (OpMed CDP), Theater Blood-Mobile (TBLD-M), and Medical Hub (MedHub).
Scope:
• Deploy globally to over five sites/units simultaneously across forward-deployed locations managed by multiple Services in EUCOM and INDOPACOM (Role 1-3) with uncertain schedule and complex infrastructure. This includes deployments from Naval vessels and field hospitals to forward resuscitative and enroute care teams
• Services & Interoperability: Full-spectrum healthcare services are in scope, including ambulatory care, emergency trauma, surgical stabilization, enroute care, behavioral health, forward resuscitative, ancillary services (pharmacy, lab, imaging), and blood inventory and management. A critical requirement is ensuring seamless data transmission through all roles of care and aggregating medical data for Command and Control Situational Awareness (C2SA) reporting.

6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

The IMS shall be developed and maintained in accordance with generally accepted schedule management practices, including Government Accountability Office (GAO) Schedule Assessment Guide best practices and Defense Contract Management Agency (DCMA) schedule quality guidance. The Government may assess the IMS using recognized schedule quality metrics to evaluate logic integrity, critical path validity, and overall schedule health. The individual projects schedule within the IMS shall not be baselined until Government review and approval is received. All changes to scope or re-baselining requests must be processed through the Government's change control process and approved by the Government Contracting Officer, Program Manager or designee. If the Government determines that the IMS does not meet contractual or quality requirements, the contractor shall correct identified deficiencies and resubmit the IMS within a timeframe specified by the Government, at no additional cost. IMS submissions that are not logic-driven, contain excessive constraints or date overrides, lack a valid critical path, or otherwise fail to support Government schedule integration and oversight may be rejected by the Government. Acceptance of the IMS does not relieve the Contractor of responsibility for maintaining schedule accuracy and realism.

B. Oral Presentation Medium: The Government intends to hold oral presentations virtually via a Microsoft (MS) Teams meeting. Offerors are not required to purchase any video teleconferencing (VTC) software in response to this solicitation and will not be reimbursed for any costs associated. Offerors must state in their submission whether they can or cannot present their oral proposal via MS Teams (see addendum to 52.212-1). If an offeror cannot present their oral proposal via MS Teams, the Government will make every reasonable attempt to accommodate the offeror via another VTC medium or voice-only telephone. By participating in the oral presentations, the offeror acknowledges that it complies with all solicitation rules and parameters, in accordance with applicable laws and statutes.

C. Recording Oral Presentations: Recording oral presentations by offerors or their representatives is strictly prohibited, notwithstanding local laws and regulations with regards to virtual meetings or voice-only telephone oral presentations. The Government reserves the right to record oral presentations. In accordance with FAR 15.102(e), the Government will maintain an official record of the oral presentation, which will be the basis of the evaluation. The official record will consist of:
1. A copy of the final presentation slides provided by the offeror.
2. The individual technical evaluation worksheets completed by each government evaluator during the presentation.
The Government's record will be considered source selection sensitive information and will be handled accordingly.

D. Exchanges During Oral Presentation: The Government intends to engage in interactive dialogue during the oral presentations. These exchanges are viewed as a component of the oral presentation itself and do not constitute discussions.

RFP chunk to analyze:
Instructions for Phase II Oral Presentation:
The Government's notification to offerors after evaluation of Phase I quote submissions will include the due date for Phase II written submissions, as well as the scheduled time for offerors oral presentations. The due date for Phase II written submissions will be prior to the scheduled oral presentations.

2. Oral Proposal. Oral proposals are limited to a maximum of 90 minutes per offeror. At the conclusion of the oral proposal, the Government will convene for up to a 30-minute internal caucus to determine if there are any questions for the offeror. After the 30-minute caucus, there will be a 30-minute session for the Government to ask the offeror questions. This dialogue is a component of the oral proposal, and offerors will not be given the opportunity to revise any part of their previously submitted written proposal, presentation slides, or submit any subsequent proposal revisions. The question-and-answer segment will be of clarifying nature only per FAR 15.306(a), which will not constitute discussions under FAR 15.306(b) and (d). Offerors may not exceed the 90-minute time limit for oral proposals.

3. Presentation Slides. Offerors must submit their presentation slides with their proposal submission for Subfactors 2.1 – 2.5 and must cover all presentation material. The slides must be received by the Contracting Officer via email no later than 48 hours prior to the scheduled presentation time. These slides will form part of the official record for evaluation. If the offeror does not include their slides, the Government will consider the offeror as non-responsive and exclude the offeror from further consideration.

4. Oral Presentation Participants. Offerors shall include a point of contact in their proposal for oral presentations, providing the point of contact's name, phone number, and email address. Each offeror is allowed four representatives at the oral presentation: three speakers (three key personnel or two key personnel and one company executive) and one silent participant.

2.0 Task Order 01 Scope

The Post Award Kickoff Conference will serve as the official launch of the NITE-STAR Capability Development MA IDIQ. The purpose is to achieve a clear and mutual understanding of contract requirements, establish communication channels, and set expectations for successful contract execution. Absolutely no changes to the contract will be made, nor contract terms renegotiated. The meeting will also serve as a forum for questions and clarifications.

2.1 Post Award Kickoff Conference

2.1.1 Each Contractor shall participate in a Post Award Kickoff Conference, to be held in Colorado Springs, CO no later than 30 days after contract award. The Post Award Kickoff Conference will be scheduled for approximately 90 minutes, conducted face-to-face, and will not include other contractors from the IDIQ contractor pool.

2.1.2 Each Contractor will be awarded a firm-fixed price TO valued at $10,000 to complete TO 01.

4. Oral Presentation Participants. Offerors shall include a point of contact in their proposal for oral presentations, providing the point of contact's name, phone number, and email address. The Government will arrange oral presentations with the point of contact for those who submit a complete package by the specified deadline. These presentations will be scheduled for approximately seven business days after the submission of the Phase 2 written proposal and slides and will be assigned randomly. Each offeror is allowed four representatives at the oral presentation: three speakers (three key personnel or two key personnel and one company executive) and one silent participant. Each company can attend only one presentation, either as a prime contractor or as a subcontractor for another offeror. Only government personnel will participate in the source selection process. Offerors who submit timely and compliant proposals will receive detailed information about the venue, media, and schedule for oral presentations. These presentations will be conducted virtually via MS Teams DoD 365-J. If there are unexpected connectivity issues, the 90-minute limit will be adjusted accordingly. Presentations cannot be conducted in person, and a Common Access Card is not required to access the platform. The Government will record the presentations and question and answer period. All costs related to the oral presentations will be the responsibility of the offeror; no costs will be reimbursed by the Government.

Example 1:
6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

Example 2:
7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

RFP chunk to analyze:
Instructions for Phase III Proof of Concept: The Government may require a PoC demonstration from selected offerors. The demonstration shall be conducted remotely via a secure, virtual environment provided by the offeror. Government personnel will not travel for this event. Offerors are solely responsible for all costs associated with the PoC demonstration, including providing the virtual environment, personnel, and all necessary equipment. The PoC will validate the integrated technical solution presented in Phase II. The test plan will be primarily focused on the requirements of Subfactor 2.5, but a successful demonstration will require the offeror to incorporate and demonstrate capabilities related to Subfactors 2.1 through 2.4. To ensure consistent evaluation, the Government will provide offerors with a comprehensive test plan and any necessary data 21 days before the scheduled demonstration. This test plan will detail the specific elements of the offeror's solution requiring validation. Offerors will be notified if they are required to participate in the PoC, which will be scheduled to take place within 30 days of that notification. The demonstration will be conducted over two consecutive days, with a maximum of eight hours per day allotted for presentation and testing.

PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

Phase II Evaluation: Offerors proceeding to Phase II will conduct an oral presentation. The Government will assign a confidence rating for the five Phase II technical subfactors. Following the Phase II evaluation, the Government will issue an advisory notification. This notification is advisory only. An offeror who receives an advisory notification not to proceed may still elect to proceed to Phase III by providing written notice to the KO within two (2) business days. Offerors who do not provide such notice will be considered to have withdrawn. Phase III Evaluation (If activated): The Government reserves the right to activate Phase III if, after the completion of Phase II evaluations, the confidence ratings of the most highly rated offerors are too close to permit a clear best-value determination. If required, selected offerors will conduct a PoC demonstration. The PoC demonstration will be evaluated on an Acceptable/Unacceptable basis. Failure to meet any single mandatory criterion in the Government's test plan will result in an 'Unacceptable' rating. 5.1.1 Web-Based Reporting System: The contractor shall support and maintain a web-based project tracking system for approximately 400 active SERDP and ESTCP projects. The incumbent contractor currently maintains the SERDP and ESTCP Management System (SEMS), which is used to track technical and financial status of projects, contract actions, document status, financial planning, and distribution. In addition, the contractor shall maintain information from past projects since FY95 within SEMS and analyze information as needed. The contractor shall adhere to security plans for SEMS according to industry best practices. The contractor shall work with the Defense Information Systems Agency (DISA) to ensure SEMS is in compliance with cybersecurity and other information management requirements. The contractor shall report findings that require action and provide recommendations of actions to the Authorizing Official. The contractor shall provide limited modifications as required by the Director to upgrade capability.

RFP chunk to analyze:
TASK 15 - Service Transition and Exit Strategy.

Subtask 15.1 - Service Transition Strategy. Effective transition coordination is critical for contract success. The contractor shall collaborate closely with the Government throughout the transition process. The Government will oversee the transfer of equipment and services between the incumbent and new contractors. To facilitate a seamless transition, the Government will provide floor space and power to the contractor for installation of their new solution while the incumbent contractor's solution remains operational. All contractor personnel transitioning current and future customer workloads residing on the SECRET/SIPR network must possess SIPR access.

Subtask 15.1.1 - Incoming Strategy Plan. The contractor shall develop and submit a transition-in strategy plan outlining the assumption of contract responsibilities upon award. This plan must comprehensively identify and encompass all tasks to be performed by the contractor, including a consolidated list of questions for, or information requests from, the Government. The plan must also project dates for future status meetings and program reviews. It must detail the call order management system and the schedule for technical training of government personnel.

Example 2:
A sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is justified under 10 USC 2304(c)(5), FAR 6.302-5, authorized or required by statute.

FAR Part 19.1306(a) specify when a sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is appropriate. This acquisition meets those circumstances, as follows:
a. The contracting officer does not have a reasonable expectation that offers would be received from two or more HUBZone small business concerns; Market research identified DH Technologies, LLC as the only HUBZone small business authorized distributor for these items;
b. The anticipated price of the contract, including options, will not exceed $6.5 million for a requirement within the NAICS codes for manufacturing or $4.5 million for a requirement, within any other NAICS code; the applicable NAICS code is 541519 so the $4.5 million limitation applies. As stated above, the estimated dollar value is $532,194 for the base year;
c. This is a new requirement that is not currently being performed by an 8(a) participant under the provisions of FAR subpart 19.8. No performance by an equal 8(a) participant was identified during market research;
d. The HUBZone small business concern has been determined to be a responsible contractor with respect to performance, DH Technologies, LLC has a history of successful performance on previous contracts for these items.

Example 3:
Market Research: In accordance with FAR Part 10, market research was conducted from 27 March 2025 to 08 April 2025. Market research on NASA SEWP identified eight (8) Service Women Owned Small Businesses (WOSBs) that are approved AWS resellers. Adequate price competition can be achieved for this requirement on NASA SEWP through WOSB competition. Therefore, it is the determination of the Contracting Officer to compete this requirement as a 100% WOSB set aside.

Basis for Contract Award. Award will be given to the responsible proposer whose proposal is determined to represent the overall best value to the Government using a best value tradeoff (BVTO) evaluation process. The offeror's technical proposal should include all information the offeror wants the Government to consider and evaluate regarding its ability to perform all required tasks and conform to all required terms and conditions. To be eligible for award, the offeror's proposal must sufficiently address all aspects of the PWS. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the offeror being ineligible for award. The Government will evaluate proposals and make an award based on the Best Value Trade-Off (BVTO) process. Award will be given to the offeror whose proposal is determined to represent the best overall value to the Government. Factors 1 and 2 are evaluated on an Acceptable/Unacceptable basis. An offeror must receive a rating of 'Acceptable' for both Factor 1 and Factor 2 to be eligible for award. For offerors deemed eligible, the award decision will be based on a best-value tradeoff where Factor 3 is more important than Factor 4. The Government intends to evaluate proposals and award a contract without holding discussions with offerors. Therefore, the offeror's initial proposal should contain its best terms from a price and technical standpoint. However, the Government reserves the right to conduct discussions if the KO determines they are necessary.

4. EVALUATION FACTORS
As part of the Technical Evaluation, the NAFI may require an oral presentation or interview from the Contractor deemed LPTA. The outcome of the oral presentation or interview may lead to an adjustment in the Contractor's Technical Evaluation Rating. The Contractor will be provided at least three (3) days advance notice, including the presentation/interview requirements and questions.

The government will evaluate RFQ submission based on the following evaluation factors:
FACTOR 1: Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW)/Technical Solution
FACTOR 2: Past Performance
FACTOR 3: Cost/Price

4.1. FACTOR 1: Contractor's Statement of Work (CSOW) (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
A Contractor's quote is deemed technically acceptable when the contractor presents a viable CSOW that is clear and include sufficient detail for effective evaluation and for substantiating the validity of stated claims. The CSOW will include at a minimum a narrative addressing each paragraph in the Statement of Objectives (SOO) (specifically Paragraphs 3-6.12). The narrative will outline the Contractor's complete understanding of the requirement and how the contractor will fulfill all requirements.

The Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW) will be incorporated into the contract at the time of award. See the SOO, for the technical aspects.

Example 2:
A sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is justified under 10 USC 2304(c)(5), FAR 6.302-5, authorized or required by statute.

FAR Part 19.1306(a) specify when a sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is appropriate. This acquisition meets those circumstances, as follows:
a. The contracting officer does not have a reasonable expectation that offers would be received from two or more HUBZone small business concerns; Market research identified DH Technologies, LLC as the only HUBZone small business authorized distributor for these items;
b. The anticipated price of the contract, including options, will not exceed $6.5 million for a requirement within the NAICS codes for manufacturing or $4.5 million for a requirement, within any other NAICS code; the applicable NAICS code is 541519 so the $4.5 million limitation applies. As stated above, the estimated dollar value is $532,194 for the base year;
c. This is a new requirement that is not currently being performed by an 8(a) participant under the provisions of FAR subpart 19.8. No performance by an equal 8(a) participant was identified during market research;
d. The HUBZone small business concern has been determined to be a responsible contractor with respect to performance, DH Technologies, LLC has a history of successful performance on previous contracts for these items.

This is a COMBINED SYNOPSIS/SOLICITATION for commercial items prepared in accordance with the information in FAR Subpart 12.6, as supplemented with the additional information included in this notice. This solicitation is advertised as a 100% SDVOSB business set-aside. This announcement constitutes the only solicitation; a written solicitation will not be issued. PAPER COPIES OF THIS SOLICITATION WILL NOT BE AVAILABLE. This combined synopsis/solicitation SHALL be posted to SAM.gov https://sam.gov/content/home). The RFQ number is 93310026Q0004. Evaluation Factor 1: Small Business Participation
The Government will evaluate the offeror's Small Business Participation submission on an "Acceptable" or "Unacceptable" basis. The criteria for an "Acceptable" rating differ for "Other Than Small Business" and "Small Business" offerors.

For the purposes of all calculations under this factor, offerors shall use $500M for the "Total Contract Value" (TCV). This TCV is for evaluation standardization only and does not represent a guarantee or ceiling.

Table 2: MQRs for this Requirement
SB 23% of the total acquisition value
SDB 5% of the total acquisition value
WOSB 5% of the total acquisition value
HUBZone 3% of the total acquisition value
SDVOSB 5% of the total acquisition value

Note: Double counting is permitted. A participation plan that reflects the required percentage of the acquisition value for any of the specific small business categories (SDB, WOSB, HUBZone, SDVOSB) will also count towards the overall 23% small business goal. The Government must be able to verify that each percentage is based on the total evaluated price.

Large business offerors are required to submit a formal Small Business Subcontracting Plan. This plan will be evaluated to determine the extent to which the offeror commits to utilizing small businesses. The plan must contain commitments, goals, and dollars that meet or exceed the Minimum Quantitative Requirements (MQRs) listed in Table 2, or provide an acceptable, detailed rationale as to why an MQR cannot be met.

PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

Example 1:
The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

Example 2:
6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

Example 3:
7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
CIRCLE will bring together integrated technical area (TA) performer teams that will develop this capability. CIRCLE performers will develop and validate computational "digital twin" models of critical illness pathophysiology capable of predicting individual critical illness trajectories and serving as the basis for both novel diagnostics and defining novel immune system control points. These digital twins should incorporate individualized, dynamic, tissue-specific patient data. These models will be capable of describing and predicting the disease trajectories of individual patients as well as patient sub-populations, explain underlying pathophysiologic processes, suggest therapeutic control points that can be leveraged to treat individual and subgroup manifestations of critical illness, and be capable of serving as the basis for simulated (in silico) clinical trials of putative therapies. In silico clinical trials are a use of computational modeling to predict how treatments interact with the human body. Simulations are not expected to substitute for real-world clinical trials. However, in silico trials can be used as a cost- and time-efficient way to make pre-trial predictions, inform the design of clinical trial protocols and inclusion/exclusion criteria, optimize dosages and treatment schedules, assess safety, and, through the use of virtual populations with varying genetics, health backgrounds, and presenting etiologies, enable mechanistic insights into disease processes, and the possibilities for personalized care. To achieve this goal most optimally, CIRCLE expects performers to be integrated, multi-component teams with expertise in clinical care of critically ill patients, animal models of critical illness, data platforms, computational modeling, and the specific intervention modalities on which the team will focus. Performer teams may include academic institutions, not-for-profits, corporate entities, or a combination thereof.

RFP chunk to analyze:
Each TA performer team will be composed of three sub-groups addressing the "Measure" (TA1), "Model" (TA2), and "Modulate" (TA3), TAs of the CIRCLE program, to be integrated, coordinated, and managed by the Team Integrator component of that team. The Team Integrator component of the CIRCLE program represents and highlights the overarching focus on integrated delivery of both research & development as well as commercialization/transition inherent to the CIRCLE program; this component is expected to play a management role and thus must be led by the performing teams' Project Leads (PLs). The Team Integrator component is responsible for assuring that all team-level milestones (section 1.6.2) are met. Areas of responsibility include:
• Within-team, cross-TA integration, and technology transition: The Team Integrator will assure that data collection under TA1 will deliver the data needed (in terms of scope, volume, accuracy, frequency/granularity, and volume) to achieve the needs of TA2 and TA3, and that TA2 develops models that will deliver the information needed by TA3 to deliver effective therapies. Team Integrators will identify TA-specific technologies and multi-TA integrations that may transition to the commercial marketplace and support the transition of integrated healthcare innovations.
• Program-wide TA integration and interaction with AP teams: Team Integrators will collaborate with the AP teams to reach consensus on the choice of data to be collected by the AP1 team. Similarly, Team Integrators will work to assure that the TA groups take advantage of common data products provided by the AP1 performer. Team Integrators will collaborate with the AP2 performer to incorporate TA2-generated models into standardized CIRCLE digital twin platform(s) for validation and future FDA evaluation.

Key Roles

Listed are the key roles the contractor shall provide at time of contract award and the general functionality of their position is listed in Appendix G. The minimum education, training, and experience required by contractor personnel (whether prime or subcontractor) to perform support tasks identified in this contract are defined in the labor category descriptions provided herein.

Additional information on these categories is found in Appendix G. Personnel in labor categories designated as key roles cannot be replaced without the approval of the government. The following are identified as key roles:

Program Manager
Systems Engineer
Facilities Manager
Security Manager

Beyond these key roles, non-key personnel categories include: Telecommunications Engineer, Cybersecurity Engineer, Systems Administrator, Information System Security Engineer (ISSE), Administrative Assistant, CAD Technician, Radio Frequency Systems Technologist, Information System Security Officer (ISSO), Logistician, Business Process Manager, Purchasing and Materials Coordinator, Project Manager, Configuration Manager, Information Technology Architect, Antenna Specialist, and Software Developer.

RFP chunk to analyze:
Thus, CIRCLE will be composed of two types of performers: 1) integrated teams each working on all three TAs, and 2) no more than three performers carrying out support and Independent Verification and Validation (IV&V) roles in three separate "APs" (AP1, AP2 and AP3) that are paired with the corresponding TAs: TA1, TA2 and TA3 (See Figure 1 and sections 1.5.2 and 1.6.4 below).

Each proposal responsive to this ISO may be of one of two types: they must either address all three TAs, "Type A", or address a single AP component, "Type B". CIRCLE is seeking a diversity of approaches for each TA, and therefore individuals and corporate units are discouraged from contributing to more than one Type A funded effort. Additionally, to avoid conflicts of interest between TA performers and AP performers, no organization, whether acting as a primary or sub-performer, may participate in both a Type A and a Type B proposal.

Proposals must address all three TAs. The proposing organization must designate an individual or individuals (typically the proposal Project Lead (PL) working with one or more administrative managers) to have the primary role of Team Integrator to coordinate the activities of groups dedicated to the three TAs, and assure that milestones are met and deliverables delivered, whether these groups are components of the proposing organization or sub-performers. All proposals must include appropriate effort on the part of the PL to coordinate each TA with AP/IV&V partners (AP1, AP2, and AP3) as indicated in sections 1.6.3, and 1.6.4 below.

Multiple awards are anticipated for TA team (Type A) performers to assure sufficient diversity in measurement, modeling, and therapeutic modulation, as well as a diversity of different etiologies of critical illness from different hospitals and regions to foster a variety of solutions that reflect the U.S. healthcare system.

The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

RFP chunk to analyze:
CIRCLE performers will focus initially on repurposing existing, FDA-approved therapies that can effect immuno-inflammatory reprogramming, ideally with tissue-specific precision, based on individualized patient data and computational models of immune system dynamics and their generative mechanisms. If justified, TA3 will support the development of novel immune-modulating approaches as a means of validating specific predictions derived from digital twin models and/or as novel therapeutics. Proposers should focus on interventions that will modulate or reprogram the immune system or other critical illness-associated pathways that will interrupt or diminish the progression of inflammatory processes and cellular stress/damage that are the hallmarks of the condition. Therapeutic interventions should be integrated with, and based on, the data obtained at the bedside and analyzed and interpreted by the computational digital twin models developed within the program. Interventions must be timely and responsive to individualized and case-specific data, incorporating knowledge of the spatial location (organ and tissue origin) of inflammatory processes, as well as the dynamics of critical illness in individual patients. Proposed interventions should be tested under the most patient-realistic conditions practical, whether in simulations, large animal/non-human primate trials, and/or human studies. To the greatest extent possible, both efficacy and safety data from pre-clinical and clinical studies should be incorporated into the databases made available for all CIRCLE performers, as well as FDA-compliant models made available through ARPA-H. CIRCLE performers will be afforded the opportunity to utilize ARPA-H resources to leverage clinical trial consortia to accelerate the process of obtaining FDA approvals for novel therapies.

6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

RFP chunk to analyze:
CIRCLE is a 5-year, 3-phase program that consists of three TAs, and three TA-associated APs as components of a distributed technical augmentation and IV&V team. These components will work together to develop, test, and integrate innovative means of assessing dynamic immune state, computational modeling, and modulation of the immune system to mitigate critical illness. Taken as a whole, CIRCLE will lead to the creation of technologies, processes and methods that can transition into the clinic and healthcare marketplace efficiently and sustainably. CIRCLE will structure work across three Phases, as follows:
• Phase I (3 years, months 1-36): Performers will develop, prototype, evaluate, and test their approaches in each of the three TAs.
• Phase II (option, 1 year, months 37-48): Performers will engage in a process of in silico and initial clinical evaluation to test and refine the integrated measurement, modeling and modulation systems. The performer is required to secure at least 5% of their Phase II budget in funding from commercialization partners in Phase II.
• Phase III (option, 1 year, months 49-60): Performers will engage in pre-transition activities including: business planning, fundraising, developing partnerships, obtaining initial FDA classification and making appropriate filings, engaging in clinical trials, refinement of devices, processes, and systems integration, user testing, etc. The performer is required to secure at least 25% of their Phase III budget in funding from commercialization partners in Phase III.
Progression to Phase II and to Phase III will depend on performance against milestones (Table 1), metrics (Table 2), and evaluation by the AP IV&V performer(s). At the end of Phase I, a down-select may occur.

3. ELIGIBILITY INFORMATION
1.8. ELIGIBLE PROPOSERS
All responsible sources capable of satisfying the Government's needs may submit a proposal in response to this ISO, except as noted below. Specifically, universities, non-profit organizations, small businesses and other than small businesses are eligible and encouraged to propose to this ISO.

1.9. PROHIBITION OF PERFORMER PARTICIPATION FROM FEDERALLY FUNDED RESEARCH AND DEVELOPMENT CENTERS (FFRDCS) AND GOVERNMENT ENTITIES
ARPA-H is primarily interested in responses to programs from commercial performers, academia, non-profit organizations, etc. In certain circumstances, FFRDCs and U.S. Government Entities will have unique capabilities that are not available to proposing teams through any other resource. Accordingly, the following principles will apply to this ISO:
(1) FFRDCs and U.S. Government entities, including federal Government employees, are not permitted to respond to this ISO as a prime or sub-performer on a proposed performer team.
(2) If an FFRDC or U.S. Government entity has a unique research idea that is within the technology scope of this ISO that they would like considered for funding, contact this email address: CIRCLE@arpa-h.gov.
(3) If an FFRDC or U.S. Government entity, including a federal Government employee, is interested in working directly with the Government team supporting the research described by this ISO, the party should contact CIRCLE@arpa-h.gov.

RFP chunk to analyze:
CIRCLE performers will create and compile datasets and methods for biological sampling from patients that represent the spatiotemporal variation of biomarkers relevant to immuno-inflammatory processes that underlie critical illness. Proposers should focus on obtaining and generating data that support mechanistic modeling of the underlying immune, inflammatory, and cellular stress/damage processes that characterize critical illness as appropriate for generating, calibrating, verifying, and validating companion digital twin simulations. To the greatest extent practical, data should come from patients as opposed to animal models (although the use of clinically realistic animal models for verification and validation of model predictions, for the evaluation of therapeutics, and the safety and effectiveness of new sampling methods is appropriate, as are data obtained from other experimental systems if justified). Proposers will identify existing data sets that can be leveraged and will propose methods for expanding and improving data resources, particularly in the areas of temporal and spatial resolution. Datasets may be used for stratification of subpopulation of critically ill patients, but the expectation is that the data should focus on mechanisms common to critical illness and be broadly relevant and applicable to critical illness of diverse proximal causes. To this end, performers will be expected to work with ARPA-H resources that will both provide access to large-scale patient data and will also incorporate performer-generated data into a common CIRCLE database that will ultimately become a public resource.

• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

10.2.1. The Contractor will provide a CSOW. The CSOW defines/explains how the contractor will meet the tasks required to fulfill the requirements of the Statement of Objectives (SOO). The Contractor will provide a written response outlining its technical solution to execute the requirement outlined in each paragraph and subparagraph in the SOO.
