The NAFI will evaluate the CSOW as part of Technical Evaluation (Evaluation Factors - LPTA). The CSOW will be incorporated into the contract award.

Example 2:
PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

Example 3:
4. EVALUATION FACTORS
As part of the Technical Evaluation, the NAFI may require an oral presentation or interview from the Contractor deemed LPTA. The outcome of the oral presentation or interview may lead to an adjustment in the Contractor's Technical Evaluation Rating. The Contractor will be provided at least three (3) days advance notice, including the presentation/interview requirements and questions.

The government will evaluate RFQ submission based on the following evaluation factors:
FACTOR 1: Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW)/Technical Solution
FACTOR 2: Past Performance
FACTOR 3: Cost/Price

4.1. FACTOR 1: Contractor's Statement of Work (CSOW) (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
A Contractor's quote is deemed technically acceptable when the contractor presents a viable CSOW that is clear and include sufficient detail for effective evaluation and for substantiating the validity of stated claims. The CSOW will include at a minimum a narrative addressing each paragraph in the Statement of Objectives (SOO) (specifically Paragraphs 3-6.12). The narrative will outline the Contractor's complete understanding of the requirement and how the contractor will fulfill all requirements.

The Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW) will be incorporated into the contract at the time of award. See the SOO, for the technical aspects.

SECTION 5 – COST/PRICE PROPOSAL INSTRUCTIONS

5.1 GENERAL
Offerors shall submit a detailed cost/price proposal in a separate volume from the technical proposal. Cost information shall not appear in the technical volume.

5.2 TOTAL CONTRACT CEILING
The total contract ceiling for this effort, inclusive of all options, shall not exceed $72,000.00. This ceiling encompasses all labor categories, materials, other direct costs (ODCs), overhead, general and administrative expenses, and profit/fee. Proposals exceeding the stated ceiling may be determined ineligible for award without further evaluation.

5.3 COST BREAKDOWN
Offerors shall provide a cost breakdown by the following Contract Line Item Numbers:

CLIN 0001 – Base Period: Data Migration and System Configuration Services (6 months)
CLIN 0002 – Option Period 1: Ongoing Technical Support and Maintenance (6 months)

For each CLIN, the offeror shall identify: labor categories, estimated hours, fully burdened hourly rates, material costs, and travel (if applicable). Travel shall not exceed $3,500.00 and must comply with the Federal Travel Regulation (FTR).

5.4 PRICE REASONABLENESS
The Government intends to evaluate price reasonableness in accordance with FAR 13.106-3. The Contracting Officer may use comparison of proposed prices, market research data, or historical pricing to determine reasonableness.

This procurement is issued by the Bureau of Indian Affairs, Office of Information Technology, in support of the Land Title and Records Office (LTRO) database modernization initiative. All responsible sources may submit quotations, which shall be considered. This is a 100% Indian Small Business Economic Enterprise (ISBEE) set-aside pursuant to the Buy Indian Act (25 U.S.C. § 47).

Requirements – Technical: The selected software must meet the following criteria: Content Management System – preference will be given to a mature, proven well-known CMS that supports multiple secure user accounts that enable specific view/report/add/edit permissions. Software should be reliable, secure and robust, in accordance with industry standards and practices. The software must be cloud based (software as a service) platform. Ability to integrate with other software solutions via an API or other methods that would allow the potential for CRM and Web Site interfaces. Software will contain an accurate and complete audit trail that ensures efficient processing. Software must be able to identify and itemize all fees; such as program, rental, transaction, and convenience fees. Must have the ability to provide total program costs to client. Software must be compatible with the County's financial software, Tyler Munis, and post transactions to the General Ledger. Software shall include the ability to implement different security access levels, and elevated admin privileges to appropriate staff. Launch Live Date – Targeted for July 1, 2026. Software must have a comprehensive storage procedure to store all documents and records in a manner that is easily retrievable by County. Software must have cybersecurity protections and company must provide cyberliability insurance coverage.

Example 3:
A sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is justified under 10 USC 2304(c)(5), FAR 6.302-5, authorized or required by statute.

FAR Part 19.1306(a) specify when a sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is appropriate. This acquisition meets those circumstances, as follows:
a. The contracting officer does not have a reasonable expectation that offers would be received from two or more HUBZone small business concerns; Market research identified DH Technologies, LLC as the only HUBZone small business authorized distributor for these items;
b. The anticipated price of the contract, including options, will not exceed $6.5 million for a requirement within the NAICS codes for manufacturing or $4.5 million for a requirement, within any other NAICS code; the applicable NAICS code is 541519 so the $4.5 million limitation applies. As stated above, the estimated dollar value is $532,194 for the base year;
c. This is a new requirement that is not currently being performed by an 8(a) participant under the provisions of FAR subpart 19.8. No performance by an equal 8(a) participant was identified during market research;
d. The HUBZone small business concern has been determined to be a responsible contractor with respect to performance, DH Technologies, LLC has a history of successful performance on previous contracts for these items.

Example 1:
How does this acquisition approach map to your Agile development approach and what incentives can the government provide to create more technical and schedule efficiencies?

DSCA is implementing SAFe agile methodology and is aligned to facilitate governance and decision making within DSCA and major IA stakeholders as per the established DSCA IT governance process.

A modern, agile product development methodology is required, with a strong emphasis on Human-Centered Design (HCD). The goal is to re-engineer and simplify processes, not just perform a "lift and shift."

Example 2:
The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

Example 3:
5.1.1 Web-Based Reporting System: The contractor shall support and maintain a web-based project tracking system for approximately 400 active SERDP and ESTCP projects. The incumbent contractor currently maintains the SERDP and ESTCP Management System (SEMS), which is used to track technical and financial status of projects, contract actions, document status, financial planning, and distribution. In addition, the contractor shall maintain information from past projects since FY95 within SEMS and analyze information as needed. The contractor shall adhere to security plans for SEMS according to industry best practices. The contractor shall work with the Defense Information Systems Agency (DISA) to ensure SEMS is in compliance with cybersecurity and other information management requirements. The contractor shall report findings that require action and provide recommendations of actions to the Authorizing Official. The contractor shall provide limited modifications as required by the Director to upgrade capability.

The Contractor shall adhere to the CA/CST practices and guidelines accordingly.

• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

C.3.8.4 Software Development and Technical Labor Support

The contractor shall provide Software Development and Technical Labor Support for the NextGen Passport Personalization Printers within the Non-Production Environment (NPE), passport operations, and support for CA service desk tickets. 

The contractor shall: 

Provide support for the Print Controller software to successfully communicate with the TDIS. 

Develop and support the application to enable Passport staff to perform Government Acceptance Testing (GAT) for personalization printers in a standalone environment independent of the Print Controller and TDIS. This application will have an easy-to-use graphical user interface (GUI) and can run on a separate PC or laptop that can be connected directly to the personalization printers. The application will be able to accept the passport print jobs in XML format, perform chip and coding, engrave on the personalization page, and print the endorsement page.

Work with the Government to establish virtual development and testing environments on the NPE.

Fix performance, security, and code issues identified by the Government in NPE. The Government plans to use static code analysis to identify issues and will work with the Contractor to determine required fixes before moving to IV&V.

Design and develop new functions based on new user stories, e.g., encryption, page refresh, and labeling.

Develop comprehensive test cases and test scenarios, and perform end to end integration, usability and performance testing working with the support site location team to ensure robustness of software, and highest quality and accurate production of passport books.

Assist in supporting GitLab updates, logging defects in ELM, and work with CA Security in scanning and remediation, and enhancing any user level documentation.

RFP chunk to analyze:
C.3.7.2 Incident Management (IcM) – Service Desk Procedures

The Contractor shall provide the Government access to a Service Desk. The Service Desk shall be responsive to the Government 24 hours a day, seven days a week. The Contractor shall document, develop, and follow Service Desk procedures using Incident Management/ Service Desk approaches as discussed in Information Technology Infrastructure Library (ITIL). The Service Desk shall act as the central point of contact between Contractor's service providers and the Government users on a day-to-day basis. It is also a focal point for incident reports and for service requests. ITIL 2011 defines an incident as: An unplanned interruption to an IT Service or reduction in the quality of an IT service. Failure of a configuration item that has not yet affected service is also an incident — for example, failure of a module in the printer that has redundant capability such as the failure of a single laser module in a two-laser module printer. ITIL Incident Management manages the lifecycle of all Incidents. The primary objective of Incident Management is to manage the return of service to users as quickly as possible.

A Service Request is a user request for information, advice, or a standard change (a pre-approved change that is low risk, relatively common and follows a procedure e.g. a password reset (ITIL v3)).

The Incident Management process shall start within one hour of the time the Government contacts the Contractor Service Desk. Within four hours from the Government notification, the Contractor shall document and implement a mitigation strategy to remedy the issue. However, in cases where an Issuance Site does not have a serviceable personalization printer the service desk must ensure that actions are taken that return the site to operation within four hours.

SECURITY REQUIREMENTS FOR U.S. PASSPORT PRINTER & MAINTENANCE CONTRACT

The Bureau of Diplomatic Security (DS), U.S. Department of State (DoS), reserves the right and authority to conduct periodic inspections of contractor facilities and to perform background investigations on the company and its management in terms of its affiliations with persons or organizations that are inimical to the security interests of the United States, as well as background investigations of company employees and inspection of contractor facilities that are necessary to ensure proper compliance with the terms of the contract. The particular area of security interest under this contract relates to the manufacturing facilities and its employees who will be involved in the manufacturing of the printers and/or the maintenance of the printers during their life cycle.

A TOP SECRET facility clearance is required for the Prime Contractor at the beginning of contractor performance.

Facility and Personnel Security Clearances

(a) A facility security clearance at the TOP SECRET level, issued by the Defense Counterintelligence and Security Agency (DCSA), is required for performance on this contract and any subsequent task orders in accordance with the DD Form 254, Department of Defense Contract Security Classification Specification.

(b) Since it will be necessary for some Contractor personnel to have access to classified material and/or to enter into areas requiring a security clearance, each Contractor employee requiring such access must have an individual security clearance issued by DCSA commensurate with the required access prior to performance. Individuals must maintain their personnel security clearance throughout the duration of employment under this award. At a minimum, personnel performing on-site maintenance at DoS locations must have a SECRET personnel clearance.

6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

Example 2:
7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

Example 3:
How does this acquisition approach map to your Agile development approach and what incentives can the government provide to create more technical and schedule efficiencies?

DSCA is implementing SAFe agile methodology and is aligned to facilitate governance and decision making within DSCA and major IA stakeholders as per the established DSCA IT governance process.

A modern, agile product development methodology is required, with a strong emphasis on Human-Centered Design (HCD). The goal is to re-engineer and simplify processes, not just perform a "lift and shift."

Answer: The successful CDM contractor should be expected to inherit codebase of this prototype.

28. Should offerors assume integration with existing DSAMS and Case Launcher prototypes, or consider clean slate architecture for the proposed solution?

Answer: Re-platform DSAMS

RFP chunk to analyze:
4. Legacy Database Architecture & Interim Compatibility: The AOI states a requirement for 'developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database'. To ensure our proposed Data Engineering and Cloud Architecture approach is optimally aligned, could the Government provide the underlying technology stack (e.g., Oracle, MS SQL), the approximate data volume, and whether the current data schema is highly normalized or primarily unstructured?
Answer: Oracle database, highly normalized, with 791 unique tables, many of which triplicated across 3 schemas (Army, Navy, Air Force). Please refer to the IM&T technical standards.

9. Does the Government require the modernized solution to perform real-time bi-directional synchronization with this legacy database, or is a one-time ingestion/migration into the new data architecture sufficient?
Answer: Real-time bi-directional synchronization until all legacy database is in a state to decommission.

63. What does 'interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database' mean in practice (read-only sync, bi-directional, batch vs near real time)?
Answer: Read/write to legacy database. Consolidation to be executed once legacy functionality is completed in modernized application and legacy application can be decommissioned.

Example 1:
Key Roles

Listed are the key roles the contractor shall provide at time of contract award and the general functionality of their position is listed in Appendix G. The minimum education, training, and experience required by contractor personnel (whether prime or subcontractor) to perform support tasks identified in this contract are defined in the labor category descriptions provided herein.

Additional information on these categories is found in Appendix G. Personnel in labor categories designated as key roles cannot be replaced without the approval of the government. The following are identified as key roles:

Program Manager
Systems Engineer
Facilities Manager
Security Manager

Beyond these key roles, non-key personnel categories include: Telecommunications Engineer, Cybersecurity Engineer, Systems Administrator, Information System Security Engineer (ISSE), Administrative Assistant, CAD Technician, Radio Frequency Systems Technologist, Information System Security Officer (ISSO), Logistician, Business Process Manager, Purchasing and Materials Coordinator, Project Manager, Configuration Manager, Information Technology Architect, Antenna Specialist, and Software Developer.

Example 2:
1.5. Place of Performance

Normal duty tasks identified within this work statement shall be accomplished at the Naval Research Laboratory, BPTF located at 10050 Blossom Point Road, Welcome, Maryland, 20693. Occasional travel may be required to government facilities or contractor facilities at remote sites both inside and outside the continental United States in order to perform ground command and control tasks as well as to maintain geographically separated remote antennas and associated infrastructure that are part of the BPTF baseline.

Due to the mission-critical nature of the work, security, operations, systems administration, and engineering functions are expected to maintain a daily onsite presence to ensure uninterrupted, real-time operational and mission support, technical integration, and compliance with operational and cybersecurity requirements.

The contractor shall submit all travel requirements (including plans, agenda, itinerary and dates) for pre-approval to the contracting officer's representative and is on a strictly cost reimbursable basis.

The contractor shall bill costs for travel in accordance with FAR 31.205-46 Travel Costs (subject to local policy & procedures).

Example 3:
To be considered for this work, the contractor must be capable of providing an MSP&A integration / innovation lab, supporting approximately 60 personnel, in the Colorado Springs, CO area that is approved (or will be within the current government fiscal year) to operate up to TS/SCI and SAP levels. The capabilities at this facility should allow multiple vendors to collaborate on analytical objectives supporting the customer(s).

2.0 Background
The Space Warfighting Analysis Center (SWAC) and the Space Security and Defense Program (SSDP) provide force design recommendations to the USSF via analytical studies, leveraging multiple vendors, government civilians, and military personnel.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

3.2 (U) Software Development
(U) The Contractor shall provide software development services to ensure the definition, design, implementation, and testing of the software to meet the functional and performance of new or augmented mission requirements while maximizing the effectiveness and efficiency. These new capabilities might include new sensor data integration or a new capability transitioned at "Initial Operational Capability (IOC)" that requires some development to reach "Full Operational Capability (FOC)" capability maturity. These services include the following:
• Sprint/Release planning
• Software coding
• Software integration
• Software unit testing and reporting (CDRL-A009, CDRL-A010)
• Software deployment to operations (CDRL-A008)
• Software enterprise/user testing

(U) Software development services shall, to the maximum extent possible, follow the established NGA Agile software development processes and procedures. This includes an Agile requirements management system using the current Scaled Agile Framework for all work performed, and the integration of Contractor development requirements (story and features) with the higher-level government requirements system (Program Epics and Solution Epics in USMS Jira on COE). The Contractor shall maintain an USMS Jira project on COE as the authoritative source of truth for all system work, ensuring that Features reflect current information and status.

(U) Software testing data (plans, procedures, and reports) shall, to the maximum extent possible, utilize modern digital engineering tools. The Test Plan shall include test objectives with traceability to user acceptance criteria at the feature level.

Normal duty tasks identified within this work statement shall be accomplished at the Naval Research Laboratory, BPTF located at 10050 Blossom Point Road, Welcome, Maryland, 20693. To be considered for this work, the contractor must be capable of providing an MSP&A integration / innovation lab, supporting approximately 60 personnel, in the Colorado Springs, CO area that is approved (or will be within the current government fiscal year) to operate up to TS/SCI and SAP levels. The capabilities at this facility should allow multiple vendors to collaborate on analytical objectives supporting the customer(s).

2.0 Background
The Space Warfighting Analysis Center (SWAC) and the Space Security and Defense Program (SSDP) provide force design recommendations to the USSF via analytical studies, leveraging multiple vendors, government civilians, and military personnel.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

Example 1:
PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

Example 2:
4. EVALUATION FACTORS
As part of the Technical Evaluation, the NAFI may require an oral presentation or interview from the Contractor deemed LPTA. The outcome of the oral presentation or interview may lead to an adjustment in the Contractor's Technical Evaluation Rating. The Contractor will be provided at least three (3) days advance notice, including the presentation/interview requirements and questions.

The government will evaluate RFQ submission based on the following evaluation factors:
FACTOR 1: Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW)/Technical Solution
FACTOR 2: Past Performance
FACTOR 3: Cost/Price

4.1. FACTOR 1: Contractor's Statement of Work (CSOW) (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
A Contractor's quote is deemed technically acceptable when the contractor presents a viable CSOW that is clear and include sufficient detail for effective evaluation and for substantiating the validity of stated claims. The CSOW will include at a minimum a narrative addressing each paragraph in the Statement of Objectives (SOO) (specifically Paragraphs 3-6.12). The narrative will outline the Contractor's complete understanding of the requirement and how the contractor will fulfill all requirements.

The Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW) will be incorporated into the contract at the time of award. See the SOO, for the technical aspects.

Example 3:
9. FILE TWO – PRICING (Reference Supplies, Services and Prices & Evaluation Factors - LPTA for Award, Price Evaluation)
9.1. File Organization
9.1.1. Table of Contents
9.1.2. List of Tables and Figures
9.1.3. Incremental and Total Price Summary (ATCH 05)
9.1.3.1. Contractor will propose unit prices for each Contract Line Item (CLIN) – IAW Supplies, Services and Prices of this solicitation. Failure to provide prices as stated above might result in rejection of the quote. Total evaluated price will include the base and all option years.

10. FILE THREE – TECHNICAL SOLUTION (Statement of Objectives (SOO) & Evaluation Factors - LPTA for Award)
10.1. File Organization
10.1.1. Table of Contents File III
10.1.2. List of Tables & Figures for File III
10.1.3. Assumptions
10.1.4. Technical Solution/ Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW)
10.2. Technical Solution/CSOW
10.2.1. The Contractor will provide a CSOW. The CSOW defines/explains how the contractor will meet the tasks required to fulfill the requirements of the Statement of Objectives (SOO). The Contractor will provide a written response outlining its technical solution to execute the requirement outlined in each paragraph and subparagraph in the SOO. "Yes," "Met," and other ambiguous responses that do not provide an actual technical solution will be deemed unacceptable. The NAFI will evaluate the CSOW as part of Technical Evaluation (Evaluation Factors - LPTA). The CSOW will be incorporated into the contract award.

RFP chunk to analyze:
B. BASIS FOR AWARD (Section M)

The award will be made to a single responsible small business Offeror whose proposal is determined to be the lowest price, technically acceptable offer (FAR 15.101-2). To receive consideration for award, an "Acceptable" rating must be achieved for the Combined Technical/Management factor (to include all sub factors) and the Past Performance factor. The Cost/Price factor will not be rated, but will be evaluated for completeness, reasonableness and for realism as specified in this solicitation. Any proposal receiving a rating of "Unacceptable" in any non-price Factor will not be eligible for award. The evaluation criteria detailing the Factors and Subfactors are contained in Section M of the solicitation and are herein incorporated by reference.

The Government reserves the right to award without discussions; therefore, offerors will be cautioned to ensure that their proposals contain all required information and are complete in ALL respects.

FACTORS AND SUB-FACTORS TO BE EVALUATED

The following evaluation factors and sub factors will be used to evaluate each proposal. Award will be made based on the lowest price of the proposal that is deemed technically acceptable.

A detailed explanation of the criteria for evaluation is set forth in the "Evaluation Approach," paragraph C of this section. During evaluation of each proposal, the Government will assign each factor/sub factor, excluding Price, an Acceptable/Unacceptable rating. Each sub factor will be evaluated to determine if the proposed approach associated with each sub factor meets or does not meet the minimum performance or capability requirements. To receive an overall "Acceptable" factor rating, all sub factors must receive an "Acceptable" rating.

A sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is justified under 10 USC 2304(c)(5), FAR 6.302-5, authorized or required by statute.

FAR Part 19.1306(a) specify when a sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is appropriate. This acquisition meets those circumstances, as follows:
a. The contracting officer does not have a reasonable expectation that offers would be received from two or more HUBZone small business concerns; Market research identified DH Technologies, LLC as the only HUBZone small business authorized distributor for these items;
b. The anticipated price of the contract, including options, will not exceed $6.5 million for a requirement within the NAICS codes for manufacturing or $4.5 million for a requirement, within any other NAICS code; the applicable NAICS code is 541519 so the $4.5 million limitation applies. As stated above, the estimated dollar value is $532,194 for the base year;
c. This is a new requirement that is not currently being performed by an 8(a) participant under the provisions of FAR subpart 19.8. No performance by an equal 8(a) participant was identified during market research;
d. The HUBZone small business concern has been determined to be a responsible contractor with respect to performance, DH Technologies, LLC has a history of successful performance on previous contracts for these items.

4. Please clarify how the government intends to evaluate how well the offeror performed on contracts submitted under Factor 2 (Past Performance), including what sources of information the Government intends to use to assess performance quality and whether offerors are required to submit additional information to support this assessment?

The basis for award is LPTA. Please refer to section C. EVALUATION APPROACH, Factor 2: Past Performance.

5. It is our understanding that Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection procedures are generally most appropriate for the acquisition of non-complex services with minimal performance risk. Section 813 of the FY17 NDAA, as amended by Section 822 of the FY18 NDAA, encourages DoD contracting officers to avoid, to the maximum extent practicable, the use of LPTA for procurements that are predominately for IT services, systems engineering and technical assistance, cybersecurity services, or other knowledge-based professional services. Given that the scope of this requirement includes complex, knowledge-based ERP, and IT functional support services as described in the PWS, would the Government consider the use of a Best Value Tradeoff source selection methodology in lieu of LPTA for this acquisition?

For this acquisition, the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) will be utilized as stated in the solicitation.

2.0 Background
The Space Warfighting Analysis Center (SWAC) and the Space Security and Defense Program (SSDP) provide force design recommendations to the USSF via analytical studies, leveraging multiple vendors, government civilians, and military personnel.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

5.1.1 Web-Based Reporting System: The contractor shall support and maintain a web-based project tracking system for approximately 400 active SERDP and ESTCP projects. The incumbent contractor currently maintains the SERDP and ESTCP Management System (SEMS), which is used to track technical and financial status of projects, contract actions, document status, financial planning, and distribution. In addition, the contractor shall maintain information from past projects since FY95 within SEMS and analyze information as needed. The contractor shall adhere to security plans for SEMS according to industry best practices. The contractor shall work with the Defense Information Systems Agency (DISA) to ensure SEMS is in compliance with cybersecurity and other information management requirements. The contractor shall report findings that require action and provide recommendations of actions to the Authorizing Official. The contractor shall provide limited modifications as required by the Director to upgrade capability.

Example 2:
The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.

RFP chunk to analyze:
PWD's GIS Unit was established in 1996 with the remit of owning and maintaining the Department's geospatial data. In 2001, the unit undertook its first major initiative by scanning, attributing, and spatially indexing the Department's 250,000 engineering drawings. This resulted in the development of PWD's Engineering Record Viewer (ERV) application, which allowed Departmental users to search for and view scanned drawings. Data conversion followed, beginning in 2004, in which PWD's linear assets were converted to GIS features, resulting in the creation of six geometric networks for water, wastewater, stormwater, raw water, high pressure fire system, and green stormwater infrastructure. These networks were incorporated into ERV, with subsequent versions of the application further expanding its functionalities and the data available to Departmental users. Today, a daily average of 250 PWD employees with diverse skillsets and requirements interact with the Department's GIS data via ERV.

PWD's GIS datasets are maintained continuously by a team of editors in the GIS Unit, tracking the full lifecycle of every asset, and are made available to users across the Department for a wide range of purposes via ERV and other applications. The data are used for hydraulic modeling; flood risk modeling and sewer planning; and linear asset planning and risk-based pipe renewal asset planning.

7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

PURPOSE: This SOP establishes guidelines for USAREUR-AF G4 Logistics Automation Division (LAD) Staging Facility operations and the supported customers for issuing and receiving of Tactical Enterprise Logistics Systems (TELS), Logistics Information Systems (LIS), and Commercial off the Shelf Information Technology (COTS IT) equipment for "UNCLASSIFIED" PM fielded U.S. Army Government property and commercial vendors. The USAREUR-AF G4 LAD Staging Facility, located in Miesau, Germany, is the centralized point in Europe for Army Program Managers dealing with TELS, LIS, COTS IT, Life Cycle Replacement (LCR), and shortages fielded to units within the USAREUR-AF Theater of Operations. Equipment is fielded and issued based on the unit's MTOE, TDA, and ATOE authorizations. This facility utilizes Global Combat Support System- Army (GCSS-Army) Property Book, the Army's supply system of record for accountability of transactions between gaining and losing UICs for TELS, LIS, and COTS IT. This ensures an accurate, auditable trail IAW DoD and HQ DAG4 policy. The USAREUR-AF G4 and G6 TELS, LIS, COTS IT system and program managers maintain fully mission capable (FMC) equipment to fill shortages and or mission requirements.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

4.1. FACTOR 1: Contractor's Statement of Work (CSOW) (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
A Contractor's quote is deemed technically acceptable when the contractor presents a viable CSOW that is clear and include sufficient detail for effective evaluation and for substantiating the validity of stated claims. Example 2:
PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

Example 1:
7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

RFP chunk to analyze:
C.4 Scope
Under this contract, the Contractor shall expand the scope and complexity of the data aggregation, anonymization, and preparation needed to enable future analyses. The contractor shall advance the level of maturity of the partnership and the trusted third-party role by deepening the level of collaboration between the partners, maturing the data-sharing platform to accelerate independent analyses for comparison, and increasing the number of participant organizations. As such, the work performed under this contract is non-severable. Specifically, the Contractor shall:
1) Expand and mature the breadth and depth of the partnership by:
a) Providing necessary governance and management to ensure success of the data trust and partnership.
b) Providing and leveraging highway safety expertise to facilitate novel research that can only be accomplished in a data trust.
c) Refining the data ingestion, anonymization, and analytical processes established to date.
d) Establishing the data architecture and governance to enable novel analyses.
2) Further develop the data analysis capabilities system by:
a) Incorporating additional federal, state, industry, etc. data sources.
b) Expanding the data trust and partnership to include additional members and sources of data.
3) Assist the government to maintain the partnership by:
a) Maintaining and updating the research roadmap.
b) Developing a sustainable data architecture and analysis.

RFP chunk to analyze:
Strategic Weapons Facility, Pacific (SWFPAC) requires in-person, instructor-led training for 15 personnel on VMware vSphere. The agency's server infrastructure and software-defined data center (SDDC) are standardized on the VMware vSphere 7 platform. This course provides the essential foundational knowledge for operating and maintaining VMware technologies within a software-defined data center environment. Because vSphere is the core virtualization platform upon which most other VMware solutions depend, this training is critical to ensuring SWFPAC personnel can effectively support, troubleshoot, and optimize the command's virtual infrastructure.

2. SCOPE
The contractor shall provide and deliver the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) authorized commercial training course, VMware vSphere: Install, Configure, Manage [V7]. The purpose of this training is to provide SWFPAC employees with the specific skills needed to manage the agency's standardized VMware virtual infrastructure.

The contractor shall provide a complete training package that includes the following:
2.1 Training Session: A single, five-day, in-person, instructor-led training session conducted onsite at SWFPAC facilities for up to fifteen (15) students.
2.2 One (1) currently certified VMware Instructor (VCI) to lead the training.

Example 1:
1.5. Place of Performance

Normal duty tasks identified within this work statement shall be accomplished at the Naval Research Laboratory, BPTF located at 10050 Blossom Point Road, Welcome, Maryland, 20693. Occasional travel may be required to government facilities or contractor facilities at remote sites both inside and outside the continental United States in order to perform ground command and control tasks as well as to maintain geographically separated remote antennas and associated infrastructure that are part of the BPTF baseline.

Due to the mission-critical nature of the work, security, operations, systems administration, and engineering functions are expected to maintain a daily onsite presence to ensure uninterrupted, real-time operational and mission support, technical integration, and compliance with operational and cybersecurity requirements.

The contractor shall submit all travel requirements (including plans, agenda, itinerary and dates) for pre-approval to the contracting officer's representative and is on a strictly cost reimbursable basis.

The contractor shall bill costs for travel in accordance with FAR 31.205-46 Travel Costs (subject to local policy & procedures).

Example 2:
To be considered for this work, the contractor must be capable of providing an MSP&A integration / innovation lab, supporting approximately 60 personnel, in the Colorado Springs, CO area that is approved (or will be within the current government fiscal year) to operate up to TS/SCI and SAP levels. The capabilities at this facility should allow multiple vendors to collaborate on analytical objectives supporting the customer(s).

2.0 Background
The Space Warfighting Analysis Center (SWAC) and the Space Security and Defense Program (SSDP) provide force design recommendations to the USSF via analytical studies, leveraging multiple vendors, government civilians, and military personnel.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

4. EVALUATION FACTORS
As part of the Technical Evaluation, the NAFI may require an oral presentation or interview from the Contractor deemed LPTA. The outcome of the oral presentation or interview may lead to an adjustment in the Contractor's Technical Evaluation Rating. The Contractor will be provided at least three (3) days advance notice, including the presentation/interview requirements and questions.

The government will evaluate RFQ submission based on the following evaluation factors:
FACTOR 1: Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW)/Technical Solution
FACTOR 2: Past Performance
FACTOR 3: Cost/Price

4.1. FACTOR 1: Contractor's Statement of Work (CSOW) (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
A Contractor's quote is deemed technically acceptable when the contractor presents a viable CSOW that is clear and include sufficient detail for effective evaluation and for substantiating the validity of stated claims. The CSOW will include at a minimum a narrative addressing each paragraph in the Statement of Objectives (SOO) (specifically Paragraphs 3-6.12). The narrative will outline the Contractor's complete understanding of the requirement and how the contractor will fulfill all requirements.

The Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW) will be incorporated into the contract at the time of award. See the SOO, for the technical aspects.

10. FILE THREE – TECHNICAL SOLUTION (Statement of Objectives (SOO) & Evaluation Factors - LPTA for Award)

The Contractor will provide a written response outlining its technical solution to execute the requirement outlined in each paragraph and subparagraph in the SOO. "Yes," "Met," and other ambiguous responses that do not provide an actual technical solution will be deemed unacceptable. The NAFI will evaluate the CSOW as part of Technical Evaluation (Evaluation Factors - LPTA). The CSOW will be incorporated into the contract award.

7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

7. Security, Privacy & Compliance Controls
The system must comply with HIPAA (Privacy, Security, and Breach Notification Rules) and the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) where education and health data intersect. SOC 2 Type II, HITRUST, or equivalent certification is preferred.
Security controls must include:
● Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) with granular permissions
● Support for SSO and federated identity standards (SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OIDC)
● Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
● Encryption at rest (AES-256+) and in transit (TLS 1.2+)
● Secure key management practices
● Immutable, searchable, and exportable audit logs for user access, record changes, and data exports
The system must support configurable consent management workflows (including CYBHI participation), field- and document-level segmentation of sensitive data, and role-based visibility controls.

5.2.1.5 Finalist Presentation Evaluation

5.2.4 Total Proposal Score

The Solicitation Coordinator will calculate the sum of the Technical Proposal scores, the Presentation scores, and the Cost Proposal scores and record the resulting number as the total score for the subject Proposal.

Example 3:
6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

5.2 Evaluation Process

The evaluation process is designed to award the contract resulting from this RFP not necessarily to the Proposer offering the lowest cost, but rather to the responsive and responsible Proposer deemed by the Institution to offer the best combination of attributes based upon the evaluation criteria. 'Responsive Proposer' is defined as a Proposer that has submitted a response that conforms in all material respects to the RFP. 'Responsible Proposer' is defined as a Proposer that has the capacity in all respects to perform fully the contract requirements, and the integrity and reliability which will assure good faith performance.

Example 3:
7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

The contractor shall adhere to security plans for SEMS according to industry best practices. RFP chunk to analyze:
PROJECT OVERVIEW
Unified School District No. 500, Wyandotte County, Kansas, c/k/a Kansas City Kansas Public Schools (KCKPS), is seeking proposals from qualified firms to provide a districtwide data dashboard solution that integrates instructional, operational, and human capital data into a single, secure, and user-friendly platform. The selected solution will serve as a centralized source of truth for District data, enabling district leaders, school administrators, and instructional staff to efficiently access timely, accurate, and actionable information to support decision-making, accountability, and continuous improvement. The solution must integrate with KCKPS's existing enterprise and instructional systems and support robust data governance, role-based access, and equity-focused analysis.

This initiative is a key component of KCKPS's ongoing efforts to modernize data access, reduce manual reporting, increase transparency, and strengthen the District's ability to monitor academic performance, operational effectiveness, and student outcomes across schools and programs.

Project Highlights:
The Districtwide Data Dashboard Solution will:
• Provide a single, integrated platform that consolidates data from multiple District systems, reducing reliance on manual data aggregation and siloed reporting.
• Support instructional decision-making through configurable dashboards for district leaders, principals, teachers, and support staff.
• Enable equity-focused analysis by making subgroup data visible, comparable, and easy to analyze across schools, programs, and student populations.
• Deliver secure, role-based access to data with strong privacy, FERPA compliance, and data governance controls.
• Support longitudinal and trend analysis, including historical data and early warning indicators.
• Improve operational and human capital insights, including staffing, attendance, budget alignment, and workforce trends.
• Provide a scalable, sustainable solution that can evolve with District needs and support future data sources and reporting requirements.

1.5. Place of Performance

Normal duty tasks identified within this work statement shall be accomplished at the Naval Research Laboratory, BPTF located at 10050 Blossom Point Road, Welcome, Maryland, 20693. Occasional travel may be required to government facilities or contractor facilities at remote sites both inside and outside the continental United States in order to perform ground command and control tasks as well as to maintain geographically separated remote antennas and associated infrastructure that are part of the BPTF baseline.

Due to the mission-critical nature of the work, security, operations, systems administration, and engineering functions are expected to maintain a daily onsite presence to ensure uninterrupted, real-time operational and mission support, technical integration, and compliance with operational and cybersecurity requirements.

The contractor shall submit all travel requirements (including plans, agenda, itinerary and dates) for pre-approval to the contracting officer's representative and is on a strictly cost reimbursable basis.

The contractor shall bill costs for travel in accordance with FAR 31.205-46 Travel Costs (subject to local policy & procedures).

Example 2:
The requirement is for continuity of IAS IT support services following a bridge contract. Services include:
- IAS System Operations (monitoring, administration, database support)
- IAS/IPP Help Desk Support
- IAS Releases, Upgrades, and Enhancements
- IAS Security and Program Management

Estimated (Base: 6 months + Option: 6 months)

Buchanan and Edwards is uniquely qualified due to:
- Continuity & Risk Mitigation: Retains critical IAS personnel (Program Manager, Functional Leads, Senior Developers) without costly and risky transition.
- Specialized Expertise: Proven experience with IAS SDLC, PRISM upgrades, Oracle DB migration, and USDA-specific interfaces (FMMI, IPP).
- Urgency & Mission Criticality: The current bridge contract awarded during a bid protest period is due to expire 2/28/2026. USDA and OCP have urgent requirements including but not limited to the second release and continued enhancement of the Requirements Planning Tool (RPT) 2.0, that necessitate the continuity of current IAS key personnel to ensure successful execution.

Example 3:
Key Roles

Listed are the key roles the contractor shall provide at time of contract award and the general functionality of their position is listed in Appendix G. The minimum education, training, and experience required by contractor personnel (whether prime or subcontractor) to perform support tasks identified in this contract are defined in the labor category descriptions provided herein.

Additional information on these categories is found in Appendix G. Personnel in labor categories designated as key roles cannot be replaced without the approval of the government. The following are identified as key roles:

Program Manager
Systems Engineer
Facilities Manager
Security Manager

Beyond these key roles, non-key personnel categories include: Telecommunications Engineer, Cybersecurity Engineer, Systems Administrator, Information System Security Engineer (ISSE), Administrative Assistant, CAD Technician, Radio Frequency Systems Technologist, Information System Security Officer (ISSO), Logistician, Business Process Manager, Purchasing and Materials Coordinator, Project Manager, Configuration Manager, Information Technology Architect, Antenna Specialist, and Software Developer.

RFP chunk to analyze:
Q17. Is performance strictly on-site in New Orleans, or may any non-classified tasks/deliverables be performed hybrid/remote?

A17. G-3/5 may entertain non-classified work in a hybrid/remote manner on a case by case and limited basis, but this will likely only be after an initial observation period and is not guaranteed.

Q18. Will responses be evaluated on a go/no-go compliance basis for mandatory qualifications (advanced school graduation and clearance/FCL requirements) before other considerations?

A18. In accordance with the Technical Evaluation Criteria posted within the solicitation, the Government will evaluate based on Price, technical acceptability of potential contractor employees and past performance.

Q19. Is this requirement new, a re-compete, or a consolidation of existing support? If new, what operational gap is it intended to fill for MARFORRES G-3/5?

A19. This is a new requirement. 1) The Future Operations Analyst fills a gap in the MARFORRES G-3/5 FOPS section where current and inconsistent manning does not allow for experienced, persistent presence to track historical decisions and section taskings. A revolving door of Majors and Staff Non-Commissioned officers have no institutional knowledge of previously litigated issues and often find themselves conducting experiential learning. The individual in this position will be expected to mentor these majors in the execution of their duties, maintain communication with outside executive agencies, and understand regulations and policies that facilitate execution of the AC/S's (O-6 level) and general officers' intent regarding GFM planning and large-scale exercise planning.

Example 1:
4. EVALUATION FACTORS
As part of the Technical Evaluation, the NAFI may require an oral presentation or interview from the Contractor deemed LPTA. The outcome of the oral presentation or interview may lead to an adjustment in the Contractor's Technical Evaluation Rating. The Contractor will be provided at least three (3) days advance notice, including the presentation/interview requirements and questions.

The government will evaluate RFQ submission based on the following evaluation factors:
FACTOR 1: Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW)/Technical Solution
FACTOR 2: Past Performance
FACTOR 3: Cost/Price

4.1. FACTOR 1: Contractor's Statement of Work (CSOW) (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
A Contractor's quote is deemed technically acceptable when the contractor presents a viable CSOW that is clear and include sufficient detail for effective evaluation and for substantiating the validity of stated claims. The CSOW will include at a minimum a narrative addressing each paragraph in the Statement of Objectives (SOO) (specifically Paragraphs 3-6.12). The narrative will outline the Contractor's complete understanding of the requirement and how the contractor will fulfill all requirements.

The Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW) will be incorporated into the contract at the time of award. See the SOO, for the technical aspects.

Example 2:
7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

Example 3:
To be considered for this work, the contractor must be capable of providing an MSP&A integration / innovation lab, supporting approximately 60 personnel, in the Colorado Springs, CO area that is approved (or will be within the current government fiscal year) to operate up to TS/SCI and SAP levels. The capabilities at this facility should allow multiple vendors to collaborate on analytical objectives supporting the customer(s).

2.0 Background
The Space Warfighting Analysis Center (SWAC) and the Space Security and Defense Program (SSDP) provide force design recommendations to the USSF via analytical studies, leveraging multiple vendors, government civilians, and military personnel.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

RFP chunk to analyze:
APPENDIX B – SECTION M: Evaluation Criteria

Proposals will be evaluated on multiple factors to ensure the best overall value for the government. As part of this assessment, the government will consider the offeror's depth and relevance of experience with Microsoft Defender and Microsoft Purview in a government M365 E5/G5 environment. This experience will be evaluated in conjunction with other technical, management, and performance considerations and will not serve as the sole basis for determination:
• Demonstrated success in securing PII and sensitive agency information
• Ability to deploy, configure, and tune Purview DLP across workloads
• Proficiency in threat detection, incident response, and endpoint protection
• Understanding of GCC-specific considerations and limitations

Higher ratings where candidates demonstrate advanced certifications, extensive hands-on experience, or documented success implementing multi-layered security architectures using Defender, Purview, and complementary tools.

D.2 Evaluator Scoring Rubric (Illustrative Weights)
Criterion Weight Indicators of Strength
Purview DLP across workloads 25% — Intent→config mapping; SITs+EDM; monthly KPIs; exfil tests; tuning cycles
Defender stack + Sentinel 20% — Runbooks, triage workflows, SIEM integration use cases
Insider Risk & Adaptive Protection 15% — IRM policies; Adaptive Protection scoping; rollout notes
GCC/GCC High/DoD awareness & licensing 15% — Clear feature differences, compliance handling for CUI/PII
Email exfil protection 10% — Subject/body/attachments; domain scoping; Policy Tips/wait-on-send
Endpoint & Browser/Domain controls 10% — Copy/paste/print/USB restrictions; sensitive domain groups; approved browsers
Documentation & Knowledge Transfer 5% — Playbooks, naming conventions, exception handling, user education

5.1.1 Web-Based Reporting System: The contractor shall support and maintain a web-based project tracking system for approximately 400 active SERDP and ESTCP projects. The incumbent contractor currently maintains the SERDP and ESTCP Management System (SEMS), which is used to track technical and financial status of projects, contract actions, document status, financial planning, and distribution. The contractor shall adhere to security plans for SEMS according to industry best practices. The contractor shall work with the Defense Information Systems Agency (DISA) to ensure SEMS is in compliance with cybersecurity and other information management requirements. The contractor shall report findings that require action and provide recommendations of actions to the Authorizing Official. The contractor shall provide limited modifications as required by the Director to upgrade capability.

This notice is issued for market research purposes only. This is not a solicitation and does not constitute a Request for Proposal (RFP) or Request for Quote (RFQ). DNFSB is not requesting pricing at this time.

DNFSB is conducting an Industry Day in support of a planned full and open competition for Information Technology (IT) Operations & Maintenance (O&M), Helpdesk, embedded cybersecurity operational support, audit/compliance services, and AI-enabled operational enhancements. The anticipated contract will replace the current contract, which concludes in September 2026.

The purpose of this Industry Day is to:
• Provide broad industry awareness of the upcoming requirement
• Present the draft Performance Work Statement (PWS)
• Validate operational assumptions and transition planning
• Obtain structured capability feedback
• Confirm commercially reasonable performance metrics and contract structure
• Ensure open, transparent engagement prior to release of a formal solicitation

Participation in this Industry Day is voluntary and is not a prerequisite for participation in any future solicitation.

Anticipated Timeline (Subject to Change)
Industry Day: March 18th, 2026
Draft Solicitation Release (Estimated): Q3 2026
Anticipated Award: Prior to September 2026

This justification is for a 3-month extension to the IPF Staff Support task order (HM047621F0272), originally awarded to Compass, Inc. The IPF Staff Support task order provides Information Technology (IT), Foundation GEOINT (FG) Subject Matter Expert (SME), and program management support to the NGA Foundation GEOINT Integrated Program Office (IPF). IPF Staff Support was originally competed on the General Services Administration (GSA) Information Technology (IT) Schedule 70 as a Small Business Set-Aside Fair Opportunity, with award made to Compass on 19 September 2019. The IPF Staff Support task order was awarded against the GSA's Federal Supply Schedule (FSS) contract number GS35F300GA as a Time & Material (T&M) contract. The Period of Performance (PoP) for the current task order is from 23 September 2019 to 22 March 2026. This justification is to extend the PoP to end on 22 June 2026 under the same terms and conditions of the GS35F300GA underlying GSA schedule contract and the current HM047621F0272 NGA task order. This extension action is necessary to complete the ongoing competition of the follow-on staff support requirement known as Mapping, Charting & Geodesy (MC&G) Staff Support.
