A sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is justified under 10 USC 2304(c)(5), FAR 6.302-5, authorized or required by statute.

FAR Part 19.1306(a) specify when a sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is appropriate. This acquisition meets those circumstances, as follows:
a. The contracting officer does not have a reasonable expectation that offers would be received from two or more HUBZone small business concerns; Market research identified DH Technologies, LLC as the only HUBZone small business authorized distributor for these items;
b. The anticipated price of the contract, including options, will not exceed $6.5 million for a requirement within the NAICS codes for manufacturing or $4.5 million for a requirement, within any other NAICS code; the applicable NAICS code is 541519 so the $4.5 million limitation applies. As stated above, the estimated dollar value is $532,194 for the base year;
c. This is a new requirement that is not currently being performed by an 8(a) participant under the provisions of FAR subpart 19.8. No performance by an equal 8(a) participant was identified during market research;
d. The HUBZone small business concern has been determined to be a responsible contractor with respect to performance, DH Technologies, LLC has a history of successful performance on previous contracts for these items.

Example 3:
Market Research: In accordance with FAR Part 10, market research was conducted from 27 March 2025 to 08 April 2025. Market research on NASA SEWP identified eight (8) Service Women Owned Small Businesses (WOSBs) that are approved AWS resellers. Adequate price competition can be achieved for this requirement on NASA SEWP through WOSB competition. Therefore, it is the determination of the Contracting Officer to compete this requirement as a 100% WOSB set aside.

10.2. Technical Solution/CSOW
10.2.1. The Contractor will provide a CSOW. The CSOW defines/explains how the contractor will meet the tasks required to fulfill the requirements of the Statement of Objectives (SOO). The Contractor will provide a written response outlining its technical solution to execute the requirement outlined in each paragraph and subparagraph in the SOO. "Yes," "Met," and other ambiguous responses that do not provide an actual technical solution will be deemed unacceptable. The NAFI will evaluate the CSOW as part of Technical Evaluation (Evaluation Factors - LPTA). The CSOW will be incorporated into the contract award.

Example 2:
4. EVALUATION FACTORS
As part of the Technical Evaluation, the NAFI may require an oral presentation or interview from the Contractor deemed LPTA. The outcome of the oral presentation or interview may lead to an adjustment in the Contractor's Technical Evaluation Rating. The Contractor will be provided at least three (3) days advance notice, including the presentation/interview requirements and questions.

The government will evaluate RFQ submission based on the following evaluation factors:
FACTOR 1: Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW)/Technical Solution
FACTOR 2: Past Performance
FACTOR 3: Cost/Price

4.1. FACTOR 1: Contractor's Statement of Work (CSOW) (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
A Contractor's quote is deemed technically acceptable when the contractor presents a viable CSOW that is clear and include sufficient detail for effective evaluation and for substantiating the validity of stated claims. The CSOW will include at a minimum a narrative addressing each paragraph in the Statement of Objectives (SOO) (specifically Paragraphs 3-6.12). The narrative will outline the Contractor's complete understanding of the requirement and how the contractor will fulfill all requirements.

The Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW) will be incorporated into the contract at the time of award. See the SOO, for the technical aspects.

Example 3:
PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

003 Addendum to 52.212-2

(e) The evaluation process shall proceed as follows:

1. Technical Capability
The Government will evaluate the Offeror's capability to successfully provide data that satisfies the requirements specified in Attachment 1, Statement of Work (SOW). The Government will evaluate the proposed data capabilities, including the ability to provide data with specifications that exceed or supplement the minimum requirements, with particular attention to Attachment 1, SOW Sections II through V.

2. Price
The Government will evaluate the Offeror's Price Proposal for completeness, reasonableness, and consistency.

(f) The Government may make an award to other than the lowest-price Offeror or the Offeror with the highest-rated technical proposal.

Order of Importance: Technical Capability is significantly more important than Price. However, Price will become more important as the technical evaluation of proposals becomes more equal. The Government will not make an award at a significantly higher price to achieve only a slightly superior technical approach.

V. PERFORMANCE SCHEDULE

The Contractor shall participate in a post-award kick-off meeting, no later than 2 weeks after award.

PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

Example 3:
6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

M.2 GENERAL
(a) This acquisition will utilize the Best Value approach for selecting an Offeror for award. The Best Value Approach is a method of selecting the proposals that represent the best value to the Government, based upon the evaluation of cost or price and other factors specified in the solicitation. Under the Best Value Approach, the Government will assess non-price factors and price factors based on the evaluation factors in this section and their relative importance, as defined in M.4, and select the proposal that represents the best value to the Government. This approach provides the opportunity for a trade-off between price and non-price factors and does not require that the awards be made to either the Offeror submitting the highest rated technical proposal or the Offeror submitting the lowest prices, although the ultimate award decision may be to either of these Offerors.

The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

2.0 Background
The Space Warfighting Analysis Center (SWAC) and the Space Security and Defense Program (SSDP) provide force design recommendations to the USSF via analytical studies, leveraging multiple vendors, government civilians, and military personnel.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

RFP chunk to analyze:
3.1 Guardian Automation Layer (GAL): The GAL will serve as the Single User Interface for Guardians and HR Practitioners to interact with military HR Data. Historically, Guardian data has been stored within disparate systems, making process improvements and automation cumbersome. With no single source of truth for Guardian records or port-of-call for completing military HR actions, practitioners and end users rely on manual workarounds to reach data needed for decision making.

The primary purpose of the Space Force GAL is to track and manage Guardians in a service without component structure throughout the military HR lifecycle. The desired solution will allow the Guardian a continuous experience within the GAL throughout the entirety of the HR lifecycle, from onboarding and career development to retirement and beyond. The GAL will be flexible, with a positive user experience based in human-centered design practices that are reflective of modern commercial capabilities.

The USSF is pursuing a solution that leverages role-based authorities within a unified interface to analyze data and create interactive dashboards. Low code/no code configurability will allow practitioners to adapt to the evolving policy and process requirements, such as PMA.

Out of the Box: Capability exists in the system without any changes required to implement
Configuration: Changes are required in the system but the ability to make the changes is a part of the system design and capabilities.
Customization: Changes are required in the system and require coding or development against the product line.

b. Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

A. PROJECT BACKGROUND:
The Township of East Brunswick ("Township") wishes to purchase a solution that provides a secure cloud fax and emails for the Township's IT Department.
Quotes must be received by the Township's Designated Contact Person, via email, mail, or hand delivery, by 11:00 a.m. prevailing time on April 8, 2026. Quotes received after 11:00 a.m. on the Deadline Date will not be considered.

B. SCOPE OF WORK:
The Township is issuing this request for quotation via a fair and open process in accordance with N.J.S.A. 19:44A-20.4 et seq.
The Township seeks to procure the following services:
Cloud-Based Fax Services
The Township seeks to procure a secure cloud fax platform that eliminates the need for analog lines and fax hardware. The services should enable seamless, compliant faxing, designed for regulated industries, ensure the highest level of privacy, audit readiness, and global data sovereignty.
Email Platform Services
The Township seeks to procure a secure file exchange email platform designed for safe, encrypted sharing of sensitive documents and information and the transmittance of large files. The platform should ensure that sensitive information remains encrypted and protected from unauthorized access.
The solution must be able to handle 1 terabit of files.

4. EVALUATION FACTORS
As part of the Technical Evaluation, the NAFI may require an oral presentation or interview from the Contractor deemed LPTA. The outcome of the oral presentation or interview may lead to an adjustment in the Contractor's Technical Evaluation Rating. The Contractor will be provided at least three (3) days advance notice, including the presentation/interview requirements and questions.

The government will evaluate RFQ submission based on the following evaluation factors:
FACTOR 1: Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW)/Technical Solution
FACTOR 2: Past Performance
FACTOR 3: Cost/Price

4.1. FACTOR 1: Contractor's Statement of Work (CSOW) (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
A Contractor's quote is deemed technically acceptable when the contractor presents a viable CSOW that is clear and include sufficient detail for effective evaluation and for substantiating the validity of stated claims. The CSOW will include at a minimum a narrative addressing each paragraph in the Statement of Objectives (SOO) (specifically Paragraphs 3-6.12). The narrative will outline the Contractor's complete understanding of the requirement and how the contractor will fulfill all requirements.

The Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW) will be incorporated into the contract at the time of award. See the SOO, for the technical aspects.

5.5.4.1 User Experience Research

User research and engagement activities are used by HUD to validate design decisions, prioritize enhancements, and ensure PD&R KMSS meets user needs.

Outputs from this section shall inform ongoing improvements to site usability, content, and functionality, as well as website transformation and redesign activities.

At a minimum, successful performance under this section includes:
• Delivery of required user research and engagement plans and reports
• Conduct of user research in accordance with HUD-approved plans
• Submission of research findings and recommendations within required timeframes
• Implementation of HUD-approved user experience and engagement improvements

The Contractor shall perform ongoing user research to assess and validate the design and value of system components. The Contractor shall provide a user research plan for Government approval within 45 calendar days of any 12-month performance period start and update the plan upon request of the Government.

Starting with the first full federal fiscal quarter of the Contract, the Contractor shall provide the Government with summary reports of user experience research findings and recommendations for user experience enhancements within 30 calendar days following the end of the fiscal quarter.

The Government will prioritize user experience enhancements based on the recommendations.

Upon approval of the Government, the Contractor shall implement ongoing enhancements consistent with user experience research throughout the term of the Contract.

7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

Example 1:
6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

Section 3 – Rough Order of Magnitude
Funding constraints require vendors to present a pricing approach that aligns with federal acquisition realities while supporting a large-scale AI tools and automations solution. Vendors shall describe their preferred pricing structure for each major cost component and identify where firm-fixed-price, labor-hour, subscription, or transaction-based models are most appropriate. The response must also address options that reduce or defer upfront government investment.

Creative Pricing Expectations
Vendors shall describe innovative pricing approaches that minimize large upfront expenditures. Examples include deferred-cost recovery, subscription-based implementation, or transaction-based pricing tied to recurring HRD processes such as payroll, training completions, or automated document generation. These models may allow the government to invest a notional amount upfront while the vendor recovers remaining costs through usage-based fees.

Migration, Setup, and Conversion Cost Requirements
Vendors shall provide rough estimates and preferred pricing structures for:
Program and project management
Requirements analysis and system configuration
Data conversion and data-load activities
Interface development and integration
Testing activities
Training for end users and administrators
Hosting and infrastructure setup
Change-management support
Expected timelines for each activity shall be included.

A sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is justified under 10 USC 2304(c)(5), FAR 6.302-5, authorized or required by statute.

FAR Part 19.1306(a) specify when a sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is appropriate. This acquisition meets those circumstances, as follows:

Example 1:
6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

Example 2:
The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

The requirement is for continuity of IAS IT support services following a bridge contract. Services include:
- IAS System Operations (monitoring, administration, database support)
- IAS/IPP Help Desk Support
- IAS Releases, Upgrades, and Enhancements
- IAS Security and Program Management

Estimated (Base: 6 months + Option: 6 months)

Buchanan and Edwards is uniquely qualified due to:
- Continuity & Risk Mitigation: Retains critical IAS personnel (Program Manager, Functional Leads, Senior Developers) without costly and risky transition.
- Specialized Expertise: Proven experience with IAS SDLC, PRISM upgrades, Oracle DB migration, and USDA-specific interfaces (FMMI, IPP).
- Urgency & Mission Criticality: The current bridge contract awarded during a bid protest period is due to expire 2/28/2026. USDA and OCP have urgent requirements including but not limited to the second release and continued enhancement of the Requirements Planning Tool (RPT) 2.0, that necessitate the continuity of current IAS key personnel to ensure successful execution.

RFP chunk to analyze:
TECHNICAL DESCRIPTION: The objective for PENCAP is the development of a network clock ensemble and the implementation of it on a VPX card capable of running pntOS. Prototype cards, networking methods, and an ensemble algorithm will be delivered to the Government for further development and testing. The algorithm will be designed to be robust in dynamic environments, continuously evaluating both local and remote timing references for stability, trustworthiness, and source validity. It will incorporate a weighting system, allowing references to enter and exit the ensemble without disrupting it, and will feature outlier rejection to discard invalid or unstable measurements, ensuring a consistently accurate time. To support the algorithm, a resilient network method will be designed to disseminate timing and stability information. This network will utilize various existing communication links on aircraft and can be configured in multiple ways, such as point-to-point or hub-and-spoke. The system will be designed to authenticate new nodes that join the network, ensuring the traceability and integrity of all connections. The ensemble algorithm and network method will be implemented on a prototype card. This will involve modifying a Commercial-Off-The-Shelf (COTS) 3U VPX card to function with pntOS. The design will emphasize open standards for both data and physical interfaces to simplify future integration and upgrades. The project will culminate in a series of simulated, lab, and flight tests to demonstrate the card's full capabilities.

The IWTP compound is located at Hill AFB Utah. The complete system shall be designed, fabricated, programmed, tested, started up, and warranted by a single supplier to ensure a single source of responsibility and support.

The system manufacturer shall supply 'factory' personnel to conduct on-site training sessions, totaling a minimum of seven business days of training for covered shifts. The initial training session shall be conducted during start-up as needed until the Owner and Engineer are satisfied that the operators are comfortable with the operation and maintenance of the system. Training shall be done on site with the owner's personnel.

Three months after the Owner commences system operation, the system manufacturer shall supply 'factory' personnel to conduct follow-up training of the Owner's personnel. The follow-up training shall be conducted on-site and consist of reviewing the operation and maintenance of the system, adding I/O to the system, modifying graphics, and modifying and adding control sequences. The Owner shall be contacted a minimum of two weeks in advance, prior to scheduling the training session to allow proper coordination.

The manufacturer shall supply 'Factory' personnel for start-up service as needed to insure satisfactory operation. Subsequent trips to the job site to correct defects shall be made at no charge to the Owner during the warranty period. Start-up shall be a planned and well-executed event minimizing downtime of the plant processes and reducing switchover to the new SCADA system.

Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.

3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

Example 1:
4. EVALUATION FACTORS
As part of the Technical Evaluation, the NAFI may require an oral presentation or interview from the Contractor deemed LPTA. The outcome of the oral presentation or interview may lead to an adjustment in the Contractor's Technical Evaluation Rating. The Contractor will be provided at least three (3) days advance notice, including the presentation/interview requirements and questions.

The government will evaluate RFQ submission based on the following evaluation factors:
FACTOR 1: Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW)/Technical Solution
FACTOR 2: Past Performance
FACTOR 3: Cost/Price

4.1. FACTOR 1: Contractor's Statement of Work (CSOW) (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
A Contractor's quote is deemed technically acceptable when the contractor presents a viable CSOW that is clear and include sufficient detail for effective evaluation and for substantiating the validity of stated claims. The CSOW will include at a minimum a narrative addressing each paragraph in the Statement of Objectives (SOO) (specifically Paragraphs 3-6.12). The narrative will outline the Contractor's complete understanding of the requirement and how the contractor will fulfill all requirements.

The Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW) will be incorporated into the contract at the time of award. See the SOO, for the technical aspects.

Example 2:
PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

The Naval Air Systems Command (NAVAIR) encourages educational institutions, Small Business Concerns (SBs), Small Disadvantaged Business concerns (SDBs), Historically Black Colleges and Universities (HBCUs), and Minority Institutions (MIs), Veteran-Owned Small Business, Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business, and Women-Owned Small Business concerns to submit research proposals for consideration. TWO STEP BAA PROCESS. NAVAIR uses a 'Two Step' BAA evaluation process. Offerors must first submit White Papers (WPs), giving an overview (as applicable) of the technical approach, risks, milestones, and a Rough Order of Magnitude (ROM) of costs. The purpose of a WP is to preclude unwarranted effort on the part of an offeror whose proposed work is not of interest. WPs should not exceed 30 pages in length, including the cost estimate. BRIEFNESS IS DESIRABLE - WPs RANGING FROM A FEW PAGES TO 15 PAGES ARE THE NORM. Cost estimates in WPs for stand-alone subsets of effort are encouraged. Offerors of those WPs found to be consistent with the intent of the BAA will be invited to submit a Formal Cost and Technical Proposal as described below. An invitation does not assure an offeror of a subsequent contract award.

To be considered for this work, the contractor must be capable of providing an MSP&A integration / innovation lab, supporting approximately 60 personnel, in the Colorado Springs, CO area that is approved (or will be within the current government fiscal year) to operate up to TS/SCI and SAP levels. The capabilities at this facility should allow multiple vendors to collaborate on analytical objectives supporting the customer(s).

2.0 Background
The Space Warfighting Analysis Center (SWAC) and the Space Security and Defense Program (SSDP) provide force design recommendations to the USSF via analytical studies, leveraging multiple vendors, government civilians, and military personnel.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

Building on these efforts, FDA is transitioning to Sentinel 3.0—a modernized, enterprise approach intended to improve scalability, timeliness, and responsiveness across FDA's postmarket safety surveillance activities. Sentinel 3.0 is structured as coordinated components that include (1) data access and acquisition, (2) a Government-developed data platform for ingestion and analytics enablement, and (3) scientific services to support study execution.

The FDA Data Platform is a Government-developed, cloud-based data and analytics environment that provides a highly secure, centralized foundation for ingesting, managing, and analyzing data in support of FDA's public health mission. The platform operates within a Federal Information Security Management Act (FISMA) High Authority to Operate (ATO) environment and is production-ready, scalable, and designed to support enterprise-wide regulatory and analytical needs.

The FDA Data Platform is capable of ingesting and managing the data types described in this PWS, including structured and semi-structured healthcare data such as commercial healthcare insurance claims, claims–EHR linked data, registry data, Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) data, and outpatient prescription data, in accordance with FDA standards for data security, privacy, and interoperability. This description is provided for situational awareness only and does not create additional requirements for the Data Broker Services Contractor beyond those explicitly stated in this PWS.

Vision Statement
The vision of this requirement is to deploy and configure an AI-powered Grant Intelligence platform to modernize and enhance HUD grant management and oversight, providing comprehensive insights, fraud detection, and compliance automation; with the same or more capabilities achieved during the 2026 HUGs Analytics pilot.
1.1 Scope of Work
The contractor will provide software capable of analyzing structured and unstructured grants data and software support to Housing and Urban Development's (HUD), capable of replicating and exceeding the results from the 2026 HUGs Analytics pilot. HUD conducted a pilot from January 2026 to April 2026 to evaluate a new automated grants payments compliance monitoring process across two program offices. The pilot demonstrated the ability to reduce manual reconciliation effort, improve data accuracy and assist with identifying fraud, waste and abuse.
This initiative builds on the methodology and outputs from the recent January 2026 'HUGS Analytics' pilot. The purpose of this requirement is to operationalize and scale the validated pilot outcomes across HUD's grants programs.

Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

Within CISA, the Office of the Chief Human Capital Officer (OCHCO) has a range of responsibilities that require human resource management support. Functional areas of support include: position classification, recruitment and staffing, workforce planning, benefits, and retirement, pay and compensation, data analytics, and policy. The strategic objective is to ensure the agency's workforce is capable of meeting future staffing needs, retaining the current workforce, and obtaining the best skills to support the agency's mission. The impact of this growth has expanded the need for Human Resource (HR) support services for human resource management and program/project management. These services are provided under one program, the Consolidated Hiring Operations and Personnel Services (CHOPS), as part of the effort to become more efficient, streamline operations, and improve business processes. In order to support the CISA OCHCO mission, the CHOPS program requires HR support, and program/project management support services from the Contractor. The Contract shall provide expert human resources program knowledge and demonstrated experience necessary to perform the full range of human resource functions and program management tasks seamlessly. To be considered for this work, the contractor must be capable of providing an MSP&A integration / innovation lab, supporting approximately 60 personnel, in the Colorado Springs, CO area that is approved (or will be within the current government fiscal year) to operate up to TS/SCI and SAP levels. The capabilities at this facility should allow multiple vendors to collaborate on analytical objectives supporting the customer(s).

2.0 Background
The Space Warfighting Analysis Center (SWAC) and the Space Security and Defense Program (SSDP) provide force design recommendations to the USSF via analytical studies, leveraging multiple vendors, government civilians, and military personnel.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

Key Roles

Listed are the key roles the contractor shall provide at time of contract award and the general functionality of their position is listed in Appendix G. The minimum education, training, and experience required by contractor personnel (whether prime or subcontractor) to perform support tasks identified in this contract are defined in the labor category descriptions provided herein.

Additional information on these categories is found in Appendix G. Personnel in labor categories designated as key roles cannot be replaced without the approval of the government. The following are identified as key roles:

Program Manager
Systems Engineer
Facilities Manager
Security Manager

Beyond these key roles, non-key personnel categories include: Telecommunications Engineer, Cybersecurity Engineer, Systems Administrator, Information System Security Engineer (ISSE), Administrative Assistant, CAD Technician, Radio Frequency Systems Technologist, Information System Security Officer (ISSO), Logistician, Business Process Manager, Purchasing and Materials Coordinator, Project Manager, Configuration Manager, Information Technology Architect, Antenna Specialist, and Software Developer.

4. EVALUATION FACTORS

Example 1:
PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

Example 2:
52.219-17 Section 8(a) Award. (Tailored)
Section 8(a) Award (Oct 2019)
(a) By execution of a contract, the Small Business Administration (SBA) agrees to the following:
(1) To furnish the supplies or services set forth in the contract according to the specifications and the terms and conditions by subcontracting with the Offeror who has been determined an eligible concern pursuant to the provisions of section 8(a) of the Small Business Act, as amended (15 U.S.C.637(a)).
(2) Except for novation agreements, delegates to the 127 MSC the responsibility for administering the contract with complete authority to take any action on behalf of the Government under the terms and conditions of the contract; provided, however that the contracting agency shall give advance notice to the SBA before it issues a final notice terminating the right of the subcontractor to proceed with further performance, either in whole or in part, under the contract.
(3) That payments to be made under the contract will be made directly to the subcontractor by the contracting activity.
(4) To notify the 127 MSC. Contracting Officer immediately upon notification by the subcontractor that the owner or owners upon whom 8(a) eligibility was based plan to relinquish ownership or control of the concern.

Requirements – Technical: The selected software must meet the following criteria: Content Management System – preference will be given to a mature, proven well-known CMS that supports multiple secure user accounts that enable specific view/report/add/edit permissions. Software should be reliable, secure and robust, in accordance with industry standards and practices. The software must be cloud based (software as a service) platform. Ability to integrate with other software solutions via an API or other methods that would allow the potential for CRM and Web Site interfaces. Software will contain an accurate and complete audit trail that ensures efficient processing. Software must be able to identify and itemize all fees; such as program, rental, transaction, and convenience fees. Must have the ability to provide total program costs to client. Software must be compatible with the County's financial software, Tyler Munis, and post transactions to the General Ledger. Software shall include the ability to implement different security access levels, and elevated admin privileges to appropriate staff. Launch Live Date – Targeted for July 1, 2026. Software must have a comprehensive storage procedure to store all documents and records in a manner that is easily retrievable by County. Software must have cybersecurity protections and company must provide cyberliability insurance coverage.

Example 1:
The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

Example 2:
Requirements – Technical: The selected software must meet the following criteria: Content Management System – preference will be given to a mature, proven well-known CMS that supports multiple secure user accounts that enable specific view/report/add/edit permissions. Software should be reliable, secure and robust, in accordance with industry standards and practices. The software must be cloud based (software as a service) platform. Ability to integrate with other software solutions via an API or other methods that would allow the potential for CRM and Web Site interfaces. Software will contain an accurate and complete audit trail that ensures efficient processing. Software must be able to identify and itemize all fees; such as program, rental, transaction, and convenience fees. Must have the ability to provide total program costs to client. Software must be compatible with the County's financial software, Tyler Munis, and post transactions to the General Ledger. Software shall include the ability to implement different security access levels, and elevated admin privileges to appropriate staff. Launch Live Date – Targeted for July 1, 2026. Software must have a comprehensive storage procedure to store all documents and records in a manner that is easily retrievable by County. Software must have cybersecurity protections and company must provide cyberliability insurance coverage.

Example 3:
The requirement is for continuity of IAS IT support services following a bridge contract. Services include:
- IAS System Operations (monitoring, administration, database support)
- IAS/IPP Help Desk Support
- IAS Releases, Upgrades, and Enhancements
- IAS Security and Program Management

Estimated (Base: 6 months + Option: 6 months)

Buchanan and Edwards is uniquely qualified due to:
- Continuity & Risk Mitigation: Retains critical IAS personnel (Program Manager, Functional Leads, Senior Developers) without costly and risky transition.
- Specialized Expertise: Proven experience with IAS SDLC, PRISM upgrades, Oracle DB migration, and USDA-specific interfaces (FMMI, IPP).
- Urgency & Mission Criticality: The current bridge contract awarded during a bid protest period is due to expire 2/28/2026. USDA and OCP have urgent requirements including but not limited to the second release and continued enhancement of the Requirements Planning Tool (RPT) 2.0, that necessitate the continuity of current IAS key personnel to ensure successful execution.

RFP chunk to analyze:
Lifecycle Leadership & Governance: Provide end-to-end management of the SchemaOne standard. This includes maintaining the framework and facilitating stakeholder alignment through the CCB. Vendors must provide a 24/7/365 on-call support structure capable of addressing critical schema failures or ingestion blockers in real-time. Intelligent Data Mapping: Deliver a scalable solution to map ingested datasets to the SchemaOne specification. The solution must ensure absolute consistency in field names and formats across disparate sources. High-Fidelity Transformation: Provide a robust mechanism to transform raw source data into SchemaOne-compliant formats with minimal latency and high-availability (HA) failover. Continuous Validation & 24/7 Health Monitoring: Implement a solution to continuously validate ingested data. This system must proactively detect irregularities and trigger automated alerts to on-call personnel, ensuring that configuration drift is corrected immediately, regardless of the hour or day.

2.0 Background
The Space Warfighting Analysis Center (SWAC) and the Space Security and Defense Program (SSDP) provide force design recommendations to the USSF via analytical studies, leveraging multiple vendors, government civilians, and military personnel.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

RFP chunk to analyze:
III. Funding Mechanism, Amounts, and Considerations

If NSF decides to continue this process following the RFI submission period, the NSF Tech Labs Launchpad may be structured as an OT or a FAR-based procurement. If continued, this process will require a separate proposal governed by the appropriate solicitation and evaluation framework, which will be informed by the information provided through this RFI process.

NSF intends to provide funding to the selected performer(s) to establish and operate the NSF Tech Labs Launchpad through Phase 0. Funding for Phase 0 support is expected to range from $1 million to $3 million. Ongoing operating support beyond Phase 0 will be provided through portable budget line items made available to individual NSF Tech Labs teams in Phase 1, which teams may utilize for whichever NSF-approved support organizations best fit their individual needs.

The NSF investment in the NSF Tech Labs Launchpad—inclusive of Phase 0 funding and Phase 1 portable resources—is expected to total up to $10 million across the life of the effort. These amounts represent aggregate program investment, not necessarily the funding allocated to any single organization.

Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

A sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is justified under 10 USC 2304(c)(5), FAR 6.302-5, authorized or required by statute.

FAR Part 19.1306(a) specify when a sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is appropriate. This acquisition meets those circumstances, as follows:
a. The contracting officer does not have a reasonable expectation that offers would be received from two or more HUBZone small business concerns; Market research identified DH Technologies, LLC as the only HUBZone small business authorized distributor for these items;
b. The anticipated price of the contract, including options, will not exceed $6.5 million for a requirement within the NAICS codes for manufacturing or $4.5 million for a requirement, within any other NAICS code; the applicable NAICS code is 541519 so the $4.5 million limitation applies. As stated above, the estimated dollar value is $532,194 for the base year;
c. This is a new requirement that is not currently being performed by an 8(a) participant under the provisions of FAR subpart 19.8. No performance by an equal 8(a) participant was identified during market research;
d. The HUBZone small business concern has been determined to be a responsible contractor with respect to performance, DH Technologies, LLC has a history of successful performance on previous contracts for these items.

Award Information

The NGA's Office of Contract Services (OCS) - Research Division (OCSR) has the authority to award a variety of instruments on behalf of NGA. Anticipated awards under this BAA may take the form of procurement contracts, grants, cooperative agreements, or other transactions (OTs) for prototypes and follow-on production.

The NGA OCSR Division reserves the right to award the type of instrument most appropriate for the effort proposed. Proposers should familiarize themselves with these instrument types and the applicable regulations before submitting an abstract or proposal. In all cases, the Government contracting officer (CO) shall have sole discretion to select the award instrument type, regardless of instrument type proposed, and to negotiate all instrument terms and conditions with selectees.

NGA anticipates multiple awards which will be subject to the availability of funds. Awards will be made to proposers whose proposals are determined to be the most advantageous to the Government.

The Government reserves the right to:
Select for negotiation all, some, one, or none of the proposals received in response to specific Topic Calls;
Make awards without discussions with proposers;
Conduct discussions with proposers if the Government determines they are necessary;
Divide awards into base and option periods;
Not execute one or more option periods, if an award is divided into a base and option periods;
Accept proposals in their entirety or to select only portions of proposals for award;
Fund awards in increments;
Request additional documentation once the award instrument has been determined (e.g., representations and certifications); and
Remove proposers from award consideration should the proposer and the Government fail to reach agreement on award terms within a reasonable time, or if the proposer fails to provide requested additional information in a timely manner.

If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

Example 2:
4. EVALUATION FACTORS
As part of the Technical Evaluation, the NAFI may require an oral presentation or interview from the Contractor deemed LPTA. The outcome of the oral presentation or interview may lead to an adjustment in the Contractor's Technical Evaluation Rating. The Contractor will be provided at least three (3) days advance notice, including the presentation/interview requirements and questions.

The government will evaluate RFQ submission based on the following evaluation factors:
FACTOR 1: Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW)/Technical Solution
FACTOR 2: Past Performance
FACTOR 3: Cost/Price

4.1. FACTOR 1: Contractor's Statement of Work (CSOW) (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
A Contractor's quote is deemed technically acceptable when the contractor presents a viable CSOW that is clear and include sufficient detail for effective evaluation and for substantiating the validity of stated claims. The CSOW will include at a minimum a narrative addressing each paragraph in the Statement of Objectives (SOO) (specifically Paragraphs 3-6.12). The narrative will outline the Contractor's complete understanding of the requirement and how the contractor will fulfill all requirements.

The Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW) will be incorporated into the contract at the time of award. See the SOO, for the technical aspects.

Step 8: Final Award(s) Decision.

Award(s) will be made to the proposer(s) whose proposal(s) are determined to be most advantageous to the Government, all criteria considered, and cost/price determined to be fair/reasonable.

Additional information regarding the planned number of awards will be released with each Topic Call.

5.3 Selection Notices

At the earliest practicable opportunity, the Government will provide a notice to each proposer advising the proposer of the disposition of their proposal. A proposer whose proposal is selected for negotiations and possible award will be contacted by a Government Contracting Officer to discuss any additional information required for award. The anticipated project start date will be determined at this time. The appropriate award document, when signed by the Government Contracting Officer, is the authorizing award document.

5.4 Handling of Source Selection Information

NGA policy is to treat all submissions as source selection sensitive information (FAR 2.101 and 3.104), and to only disclose their contents to authorized personnel. Restrictive notices notwithstanding, submissions may be handled by support contractors for administrative purposes and/or to assist with technical evaluation. All NGA support contractors performing this role are expressly prohibited from performing NGA-sponsored technical research, and are bound by appropriate nondisclosure agreements. Subject to the restrictions set forth in FAR 37.203(d), NGA may also request input on technical aspects of the proposals from other non-Government consultants/experts who are strictly bound by the appropriate nondisclosure requirements.

6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

FACTOR 3: Cost/Price

4.1. FACTOR 1: Contractor's Statement of Work (CSOW) (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
A Contractor's quote is deemed technically acceptable when the contractor presents a viable CSOW that is clear and include sufficient detail for effective evaluation and for substantiating the validity of stated claims. The CSOW will include at a minimum a narrative addressing each paragraph in the Statement of Objectives (SOO) (specifically Paragraphs 3-6.12). The narrative will outline the Contractor's complete understanding of the requirement and how the contractor will fulfill all requirements.

The Contractor Statement of Work (CSOW) will be incorporated into the contract at the time of award. See the SOO, for the technical aspects.

The contracting officer determines that the anticipated price will be fair and reasonable based on market research from similar types of efforts with similar labor categories and historical prices for similar recent INNOVIM Task Orders. Prior to award of the proposed task order, price information shall be obtained from the contractor, and a price analysis will be performed and documented to sufficiently determine that the price to the Government of the proposed acquisition will be fair and reasonable.

Any other facts supporting the justification.
If we cannot keep INNOVIM as the logical follow-on to bridge this current contract, then SWO would lack the staff that have the expertise and access to work in a joint program, that is in a critical period of its projects. While SWFO-L1, now SOLAR-1, is winding down and completing activities to transition, it would be a failure for SWO if we do not have the experts to help transition property, complete post-launch communications that are highly visible for SWO, NESDIS and NOAA. SWO needs INNOVIM to continue to provide expertise necessary to continue critical property activities ahead of satellite operation handover. SWO would also lack the expertise in communications to help draft, coordinate and implement important messaging to the space weather community, as well as the public.

5.1.1 Web-Based Reporting System: The contractor shall support and maintain a web-based project tracking system for approximately 400 active SERDP and ESTCP projects. The incumbent contractor currently maintains the SERDP and ESTCP Management System (SEMS), which is used to track technical and financial status of projects, contract actions, document status, financial planning, and distribution. In addition, the contractor shall maintain information from past projects since FY95 within SEMS and analyze information as needed. The contractor shall adhere to security plans for SEMS according to industry best practices. The contractor shall work with the Defense Information Systems Agency (DISA) to ensure SEMS is in compliance with cybersecurity and other information management requirements. The contractor shall report findings that require action and provide recommendations of actions to the Authorizing Official. The contractor shall provide limited modifications as required by the Director to upgrade capability.

PHASE 1 – VIDEO SURVEILLANCE SYSTEM REPLACEMENT AND EXPANSION

1. Existing Conditions
The facility currently contains approximately 750 physical camera devices, consisting of:
• Approximately 420 active operational cameras
• Approximately 320 legacy cameras that are no longer powered or connected
Upon completion of Phase 1, the facility shall have approximately 468 operational IP cameras.

2. Removal of Legacy Equipment
The Vendor shall:
• Remove approximately 320 inactive legacy cameras
• Remove abandoned cabling where feasible
• Remove unused power supplies and related head-end equipment
• Seal and repair penetrations in security walls or ceilings
• Properly dispose of equipment in accordance with applicable regulations

3. Replacement of Active Cameras
The Vendor shall:
• Replace approximately 420 currently operational cameras with new IP-based cameras
• Test and certify any reused Category 5/ Category 6 cabling
• Replace any cabling that fails certification

4. New Camera Installations
• Install forty-eight (48) additional IP-based cameras in identified blind-spot areas

PHASE 2 – ELECTRONIC SECURITY AND CONTROL SYSTEM MODERNIZATION
Modernization of head-end equipment, door controls, access control, intercoms, duress alarms, and network architecture

1. Security System Head-End Replacement
• Replace security servers, PLC processors, I/O modules, dedicated switches, graphical interfaces, and redundant UPS
• UL-listed for detention
• Fail-safe/secure per NFPA 101
• Integrate fire alarm per NFPA 72
• Maintain operation during single-point failure

Key Roles

Listed are the key roles the contractor shall provide at time of contract award and the general functionality of their position is listed in Appendix G. The minimum education, training, and experience required by contractor personnel (whether prime or subcontractor) to perform support tasks identified in this contract are defined in the labor category descriptions provided herein.

Additional information on these categories is found in Appendix G. Personnel in labor categories designated as key roles cannot be replaced without the approval of the government. The following are identified as key roles:

Program Manager
Systems Engineer
Facilities Manager
Security Manager

Beyond these key roles, non-key personnel categories include: Telecommunications Engineer, Cybersecurity Engineer, Systems Administrator, Information System Security Engineer (ISSE), Administrative Assistant, CAD Technician, Radio Frequency Systems Technologist, Information System Security Officer (ISSO), Logistician, Business Process Manager, Purchasing and Materials Coordinator, Project Manager, Configuration Manager, Information Technology Architect, Antenna Specialist, and Software Developer.

Example 3:
Requirements – Technical: The selected software must meet the following criteria: Content Management System – preference will be given to a mature, proven well-known CMS that supports multiple secure user accounts that enable specific view/report/add/edit permissions. Software should be reliable, secure and robust, in accordance with industry standards and practices. The software must be cloud based (software as a service) platform. Ability to integrate with other software solutions via an API or other methods that would allow the potential for CRM and Web Site interfaces. Software will contain an accurate and complete audit trail that ensures efficient processing. Software must be able to identify and itemize all fees; such as program, rental, transaction, and convenience fees. Must have the ability to provide total program costs to client. Software must be compatible with the County's financial software, Tyler Munis, and post transactions to the General Ledger. Software shall include the ability to implement different security access levels, and elevated admin privileges to appropriate staff. Launch Live Date – Targeted for July 1, 2026. Software must have a comprehensive storage procedure to store all documents and records in a manner that is easily retrievable by County. Software must have cybersecurity protections and company must provide cyberliability insurance coverage.

V. QUALIFICATIONS OF OFFERORS:
To be considered for the award of this contract, the Offeror must meet the following minimum qualifications:
1. The offeror must provide a list of all current security control system integration contracts.
2. The offeror must provide a list of all projects held during the past 5 years, with the projects in the Commonwealth of Virginia highlighted. The list is to include facility name, address, telephone number, as well as the facility contact person's name, title, email address, total project cost, offeror's contract amount, completion date, and a list of employees involved in the completion of the project.
3. The offeror must have the ability and commitment to have the security control system Phase 1 installed and operational no later than June 30, 2027. Phase 2, if funds are appropriated by June 30, 2028.
4. The offeror must have a sufficient number of trained and manufacturer-certified technicians to provide routine and emergency repair services 24 hours per day, year-round for each product line provided as part of the security control system upgrade.
5. The offeror shall certify that it has the competence, business organization, and financial resources to successfully perform the contemplated work.

Firm shall provide resumes of individual(s) to be assigned to the project, with emphasis to be placed on experience with renovation and construction of similar size and type of facilities.

RFP chunk to analyze:
IV. SCOPE OF SERVICES
PRICING, FUNDING CONTINGENCY, AND CONTRACT TERMS

1. Phased Contract Structure
This project shall be awarded as a two-phase contract.
Phase 1 shall commence July 1, 2026 and shall achieve completion no later than June 30, 2027.
Phase 2 is contingent upon funding approval by the Rappahannock Regional Jail Authority in January 2027. If funding is approved, Phase 2 shall commence July 1, 2027, and shall achieve completion no later than June 30, 2028.
The Authority reserves the right, at its sole discretion, to authorize Phase 2, modify its scope, or decline to proceed. No guarantee of Phase 2 award is expressed or implied.
Written authorization from the Authority shall be required prior to commencement of Phase 2 work.

The contract period shall be; Phase 1 shall begin July 1, 2026 with installation of the Camera System by June 30, 2027 to include a two-year contract for system maintenance. Upon availability of funds, Phase 2 shall begin July 1, 2027 with installation of the Security Control System by June 30, 2028. This phase shall include a one-year contract for system maintenance. Upon completion of the full scope of the project, a combined five-year contract for system maintenance shall begin to run July 1, 2028 and end June 30, 2033.
